output: Thank you very much, Mr. President. I warmly welcome the approach outlined in the White Paper, which seeks to identify the root causes of malnutrition and obesity and the resulting diseases.
Unfortunately, in European food policy, regardless of whether we discuss nerve profiles, food labeling, or certain aspects in the White Paper, we lapse into a very one-sided approach.
We are indeed attempting to solve the problems we have in Europe through product policy.
And I believe it is fundamentally flawed policy to assume that there are good and bad foods.
Many amendment proposals illustrate this. It does not exist.
There are only good and bad, or likewise balanced and unbalanced, dietary patterns.
That should be our approach.
The causes of incorrect nutrition are multifaceted.

Attempting to address this by trying to guide consumers through product labeling, or even by imposing advertising bans or regulations, or by applying different VAT rates, will not bring us to our goal.

I believe everything we are doing here should, under the heading of what we can do to encourage consumers to reflect on their eating habits, be guided by the question: what can we do to motivate consumers to think about their dietary habits?
A traffic light label suggesting to him that I am now facing a good or bad product strikes me as an instrument of disenfranchisement rather than one of education and enlightenment.
Therefore, I wish for a balanced approach here and the recognition that relying solely on product policy will not advance us further. Thank you.
Madam President, Madam Vice-President, dear colleagues, the Committee on Budgetary Control has no new topics.
We are satisfied with the old issues and would also be satisfied with progress on the old topics.
Five points at the forefront. Our urgent appeal for support for Romania and Bulgaria in building the rule of law, the judiciary, and financial oversight.
We do not want to be in the same place two years from now as we are today, and that is why this point is extremely important to us.
The second point, the reliability statement for the overall budget pursuant to the graduated control procedure, including the national audit institutions.
It would be desirable if, in the final six months remaining to the Commission, we could achieve decisive progress here, or at least agree on a common path.
One is expected to be modest as a household auditor.
The third point would be simplification
We must provide greater support to the lower levels of administration in Member States in implementing EU law.
It becomes apparent that they are entirely overwhelmed by large parts of EU law. This is why the error rates are so high.
The fourth point, foreign aid, here I would like to focus particularly on Kosovo and the issue of cooperation with the United Nations.
The Committee on Budgetary Control has been to Kosovo, and I would like to announce now that Kosovo will play a certain role in the discharge procedure.
I believe we have so far remained far below the potential of the European Union, and that we urgently need to catch up here.
The fifth point would be improving cooperation among member states in combating fraud.
We have homework here, primarily for the ladies and gentlemen of the council.

The Parliament will vote on the legal basis, the regulation on fraud prevention, in November, and then we will have to jointly consider how we can continue if we wish to achieve better results in this area.
And that has been, at least until now, our shared wish.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. India and Europe need more and better contacts with each other at all levels, particularly at the level of so-called ordinary people.
Business travelers, tourists, students, and so on. A brief note on this. It also has significance for the Commission's legislative programme, which we discussed previously.
In global air traffic, we need effective safety regulations—ones that achieve real results, not merely symbolic measures.
It would therefore be a reasonable, truly necessary measure to finally repeal the senseless liquid regulations and come up with something better.
I voted in favor of the resolution because it constitutes a clear call on the Belarusian government to finally respect human rights.

It is disappointing that the hopes for a democratic development for the people of this last European dictatorship were not fulfilled by the questionable, indeed fraudulent, parliamentary elections of September.
The same applies to the strategic suppression of the opposition and civil society.

At the same time, today we have called on the Council and the Commission to facilitate and strengthen contacts between peoples and to democratize the country by seriously considering the possibility of
Reducing visa fees for Belarusian citizens when entering the Schengen Area, in order to prevent the growing isolation of Belarus and its citizens.
First and foremost, I wish to protest against the spirit of a statement heard just a few minutes ago, claiming that foreigners would flood our labor market.
And secondly, I would have preferred a clearer, more explicit report from the employment committee.
Trust in the social cohesive power of the European Union can only be achieved if fundamental social rights are established as primary European law.

We should send a stronger signal to the Council, the Commission, the Member States, and the ECJ, and not be content with merely demanding a balance between fundamental rights and internal market freedoms and free movement.
Thus, we are changing nothing. Social rights, like civil liberties, are human rights that must not be restricted by the free movement provisions of the internal market.
And it is about defending and improving the European social model. And it is high time to introduce a social progress clause as a binding protocol to the existing EU treaties.
And it is time to amend the Posting of Workers Directive so that pay and minimum standards requirements may no longer be limited to minimum requirements. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to join the many colleagues who have already rightly expressed their gratitude to the rapporteur, Doris Back.
We have someone with her who, hopefully, will not actually shed blood, but who has invested a great deal of effort and dedication into this space.
I am pleased with her and the colleagues who have also said so, that some progress has been made, also in the field of police cooperation.
This is important in order for actual life on the ground to function properly.

I still believe, however, that we must continually remind our partners in Bosnia and Herzegovina that an immense amount must still be contributed by them, from their side, and indeed more than is currently being done in certain areas.
Internal cooperation, and the willingness to engage in this internal cooperation within ethnic groups, is something we must continually demand.
It's not the case that everyone tries to go their own way.
and the integration into regional cooperation in the Western Balkans is also important.
Thank you very much, Madam President. The distance between the European Union and its citizens must not become as great as it has become.
We must ensure that it is reduced.
In this context, the ombudsman has an important role, particularly when and where he performs it well.
It is particularly important that we can count on the ombudsman's commitment and discretion.
The issue is that we should not raise expectations that nobody can fulfill.

But it is also about ensuring that wherever shortcomings have arisen within the administration of European institutions, these are addressed and, wherever possible, reasonable solutions are sought here that can then reliably guarantee and satisfy the citizen.
The previous practice has shown that the Ombudsman takes on this task seriously and performs it well.
We can hope that this will continue to be the case, and we thank him and the rapporteur for this report.
Thank you very much.
Dear colleagues, ten years after the realization of the monetary union, Europe is facing a severe crisis.
Banks collapse or are propped up with billions from the state budget. Sales collapse, and millions of people fear for their jobs and their future.
But not only has the market failed; the prevailing politics also seem unable to learn from its mistakes.
We believe – and you are unlikely to distinguish yourself particularly with such interim remarks – that serious errors were made in the design of the Economic and Monetary Union.
Such a mistake is the structural disintegration of monetary and fiscal policy. One cannot establish a common currency without simultaneously harmonizing tax and expenditure policies at least in part.
I believe the economic imbalances within the eurozone have greatly intensified.
And what we actually need now is better coordination of economic and, above all, tax policy.
We need effective measures against tax dumping. Furthermore, tax havens must be closed down, and capital flows must finally be brought under control.
The second major error lies in the design of the Stability and Growth Pact.

Anyone who, in times like these, considers fiscal consolidation unavoidable evidently lives in a different world. Nothing would be more disastrous than responding to this economic crisis right now with public spending cuts.
The Stability Pact has clearly demonstrated its failure.
It should be replaced by an integrated European strategy for solidarity and sustainable development.
In our view, a necessary step is an investment offensive aimed at renewing public infrastructure and improving the situation of socially disadvantaged people across Europe.
A third error lies in the design of the European Central Bank itself, which is not subject to democratic oversight and is solely committed to the goal of price stability.

We advocate for democratic oversight of the European Central Bank. Furthermore, we demand a revision of the ECB's monetary policy mandate, requiring that in the future growth and employment be promoted on an equal footing with price stability.
The current crisis also presents an opportunity for profound reforms of the European monetary and financial architecture.
This opportunity should not be recklessly squandered.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner, President-in-Office of the Council, esteemed colleagues. Indeed, the automotive industry is in crisis, and there are many reasons for this.
In particular, the financial crisis has further intensified the crisis in this sector, and currently, efforts are underway in the member states to develop solutions.
The European automotive industry is a key industry.
And therefore, we also need European solutions for this key European industry in order to overcome the crisis.
Nearly all automobile manufacturers have European structures.

In order to make the necessary investments in environmentally friendly vehicles—something that has been mentioned several times today—and in environmentally sound and fuel-efficient technologies, automobile manufacturers require public guarantees or low-interest loans from the European Investment Bank.
But the supplier industry, which is very important to me, must not be overlooked in this context.
Especially in the supply industry, it is small and medium-sized enterprises that create jobs and drive innovation.
But what can the European Union do to protect this key industry?
At the previous plenary session, the Council President made a proposal to protect key industries.
A good suggestion, as I see it. If one consistently carries this idea, this proposal, to its conclusion, it means a Volkswagen Act for the entire European automotive industry.
and this would certainly be a useful instrument
However, this protective instrument is currently being challenged by the European Commission.
And with my question to the Commission: Will the European Commission stick to its lawsuit despite the crisis in the automotive industry? Or will it now change course in light of the crisis?
Dear Mr. President, Mr. President of the European Court of Auditors, Vice-President of the European Commission, dear colleagues, it has now become customary to observe that
may not be granted, as a declaration of reliability cannot be provided once again regarding the relationship between expenditures at the European level and administration at the national level.

I recall conversations with the former President of the Court of Auditors, Professor Friedmann, who once told me that, given the existing structures, it is absolutely impossible to provide a declaration of reliability.
Therefore, we should already begin considering how this instrument can be given substance in such a way that the objective of a reliability declaration, if justified, can indeed be achieved.
And there we must naturally distinguish a few things from one another from time to time.
First, a budget containing approximately 95% subsidies is far more susceptible to fraud than a national, regional, or local budget.

A subsidy budget, which is primarily managed by the member states—precisely those that have demanded significantly greater autonomy from us during the new financial period, as the criticism from the previous funding period had been that too much was centrally controlled.
This naturally also means that responsibility for the funds must be assumed at the regional and national levels.
And in the third thematic area, I would like to briefly address only,
We also need to learn how to distinguish between fraud and wastefulness.
These are also things that are always gladly thrown together
I also get annoyed when EU funds are used to support things that are not absolutely necessary.
But that was not fraud; it was wastefulness.

And therefore, those responsible for the projects—particularly in the field of structural policy—have the Member States also bear responsibility for ensuring that funds are not wasted and that only projects receiving support are those that truly generate added value for the region.
Therefore, we should certainly consider from time to time shifting parts of structural policy toward interest-free loans.
After all, if member states have to repay, they only support what they actually need. Many thanks.
It is not just me who finds it difficult to join in the growing self-congratulation by European policymakers regarding the handling of the financial market crisis.
Because if Europe were, if this EU were a functioning democracy, one would first have to raise the question of responsibility and how all of this could have come to pass.
It is simply not true that all the problems we currently face constitute a tsunami, something inevitable by natural law, but rather they are man-made.
And given all the regulatory zeal with which this Union has been characterized, one wonders why action was not taken when banks began crossing national borders on a significant scale.
Why was no action taken despite the relevant warnings, as derivatives had so insidiously crept in?
I clearly remember that Nobel laureate Josef Stieglitz was here and addressed all these points.
There was silence, there was a taboo.
One would have to start there, because recognizing one's own mistakes holds the key to resolving issues in the future.
Thank you, Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner. My question concerns your opinion on what you think about Iceland and Hungary introducing the euro as a second currency.
Do you believe that this could solve the problem facing these two countries?
The second question is whether you believe it would be possible to introduce a risk premium for derivatives, which would then be used to strengthen banks' equity capital and
My third question is, do you not believe that a reduction in value-added tax would accordingly support imports, particularly from China and India?
wouldn't it be more reasonable to consider tax reductions for our businesses and our workers, to provide investment bonuses, and to accordingly adjust the progressive depreciation?

The central point is that low-value goods are increased by a factor of 10, so that fictitious profits are not taxed, the money remains within the enterprises, and thus wages can also be paid more adequately.
Thank you, Madam President. The comprehensive report illustrates how many human rights violations occur worldwide on a daily basis.
This is particularly alarming 60 years after the proclamation of the Charter of Human Rights and calls upon us to intensify our efforts even further.
For this reason as well, I would like to advocate establishing the current Subcommittee on Human Rights as a main committee.
Today we have awarded the Sakharov Prize to Hu Jia, who advocates for human rights and the environment in China,
However, since he is incarcerated, he was unable to take him up in person.
Shame on China! And tomorrow, a debate will take place here on human rights violations in Russia.
In our relations with these two countries, we must ensure that human rights, above all else, are given a higher priority than economic interests.
We demand freedom for Hujia in China and freedom for Mikhail Khodorkovsky in Russia.

Mr. President, esteemed members of the Council, Commissioner, dear colleagues, as rapporteur for the Anti-Fraud Office, one makes interesting observations, such as the fact that everyone talks about combating fraud, yet nobody actually does anything.
Or the experience that member states meticulously calculate their payments to the European Union with sharp pencils, but when spending this money, they put on their extravagant trousers
and even make it easier for fraudsters. For example, by the fact that it still takes an average of 36 months before irregularities are reported to the European Union at all.
The European Parliament does not agree with this.
We aim for an efficient fraud prevention strategy that also includes prevention.
We want the member states to make fraud prevention a priority issue and for us to discuss together the needs and challenges involved.
We aim to foster greater interest among national judicial authorities in further pursuing OLA mediation outcomes.
With crooked routes, there must no longer be lawless zones lasting longer than before.
We need to achieve parity between European money and national money.
We want an independent authority equipped with the necessary resources and legal basis to fulfill its tasks.
And we want an agency that can cooperate silently and efficiently with the member states.
Our response to legal basis 1073-99 covers all these points.
We advocate for further developing this regulation as the core of the Anti-Fraud Office together with the Council.
We urge the Council to incorporate our comments. We urge the Council to initiate discussions on the further development of this regulation.
We want to bring Olaf's problems to a solution.
And I thank all the factions of the House for supporting this in a resolution that we will vote on tomorrow.
I appeal to the French and Czech Presidencies of the Council to pick up the ball thrown by Parliament and pass it back.
We have also incorporated solutions in our proposals that were developed and submitted by the Council's working group.
On our part, I would like to express our interest in a swift conclusion of this regulation and assure you of our constructive willingness to discuss it based on our proposals.
The consolidation of all Olaf legal bases, as the Council has envisaged, would entail another considerable loss of time with an uncertain outcome.
Olaf is celebrating his tenth birthday soon.
A consolidation means another ten years without a better legal basis.
We take the fight against fraud far too seriously for that.
Therefore, we want to now realize what is feasible, in cooperation with the Council.
Many thanks.
Mr. President, I would like to take up a remark made earlier by my Austrian colleague, Paul Rübig.

We spend less than one percent of the European gross domestic product on Europe. At the same time, we demand that the European Union take on ever more tasks for an ever-increasing number of member states.
This budget cannot possibly work out. It is therefore truly unbelievable that we have been pursuing austerity measures for many years. Not only in the frugal management of sales administration,
not to use our funds, but essentially to enable finance ministers to collect money again by year-end and have it flow into their national budgets.
We have enough new tasks for which we should spend our money.
We also have tasks that we have not adequately funded financially for years.
I believe the EU's information budgets should be significantly increased if we truly wish to connect European citizens with Europe.
And there are numerous tasks in the field of student and apprentice exchange that Europe could undertake here.
progress is also advancing in the justice sector, a traditionally state-exclusive domain.
This reflects the changed living conditions of people in our Union.
But, and this remark has already been made several times, integration must not extend too far into traditionally developed structures, particularly those that are functioning effectively.
Not everything may be swept under the rug.
Public documents and public notaries vary considerably in many member states.
In Austria, but not only here, the notary public as a public records office has a long tradition and maintains a high level of security and trust.
This must not be put at risk unnecessarily, simply because such professional groups do not exist elsewhere.
Therefore, integration and mutual openness, yes, but with moderation.
Thank you very much, Madam President. Esteemed colleagues, plant protection products have been and remain essential tools in agricultural production.
Farmers require a minimum selection of plant protection products, as this concerns the protection of the domestic cultural landscape, high-quality nutrition, and the preservation of resources.
We often conduct discussions about chemicals on a highly irrational level.
To obtain reasonable results, however, we must think and act rationally.

The consequences of the original parliamentary position would have been fatal. A broad ban on plant protection products would have led to lower agricultural yields and, ultimately, rising food prices.
Therefore, I am glad that a barely acceptable compromise was reached in the trilogue negotiations.
It is welcome that we have retained the three-zone regulation, albeit with significant exceptions for the member states.

A courageous step toward a consistent internal market solution would look different at this juncture. Rather than anticipating a final definition of endocrine-disrupting substances, we should adhere to acceptable compromises and genuinely allow the European Commission the full four years to present a scientifically sound definition.
Otherwise, we would be stretching the precautionary principle beyond its intended scope at this point.
Lastly, I regret that we are not taking any initiative to undertake a follow-up assessment of the agreement now reached. Thank you once again.
The last time such expressions of opinion occurred during a visit by a head of government or head of state, the President of this House intervened decisively.
At that time, it concerned an issue that was personally important to him, namely the Lisbon Treaty.

If today the president, the democratically elected president of a country, speaks and is severely interrupted and disturbed by representatives from the socialist side, the president does not intervene.
Do you know what this is?
This is not democracy; it is arbitrariness, and I demand that appropriate measures be taken.
Children and politics—rarely do the interests of citizens and politicians diverge so greatly.
The issue of children ranks highest among citizens throughout Europe.
For politicians, children's concerns rank far down the list of priorities, as we can again observe here.
I know of no country that has a dedicated children's ministry. Likewise, within the Commission there is no specific representative for the interests of our youngest citizens.
In parliament, the issue of children is scattered across all committees.
Therefore, I would like to particularly highlight at this point that the Commission is today addressing childcare facilities and the Barcelona targets.
Thank you. In our parliamentary working group on the Quality of Childhood, we have observed the following: Our children live in a very complex world.
The future of a person who is a child here, now, and today is completely uncertain.
A child today can climb all the way to the top of the social ladder or fall all the way to the bottom.
It can remain within its own cultural context or seek a new one.
It may follow a similar life path as its parents or do exactly the opposite.
One may marry someone of the opposite or one's own gender.
All of this was completely different more than 50 years ago, when we brought the united Europe into the world.
The diversity that characterizes us Europeans has become a defining factor in the lives of children.
The concept of diversity must therefore be integrated into the pedagogy of childcare workers and teachers.
This is still taken into account far too little at present.
European childcare facilities and schools are struggling with the challenge of integration.
In matters of integration, we must press the reset button and go back to the beginning.
Let us approach this topic once again entirely without prejudice, in keeping with the motto Unity through Diversity.

And furthermore, I urge all European Parliamentarians who follow me not only to advocate for gender mainstreaming, but also to fight for child mainstreaming—especially at this time of global financial crisis—because otherwise we will burden our children with immense debt. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I will keep this brief.
I would like to sincerely thank you for these constructive contributions to the debate that I have heard here.
I would like to convey just two messages before concluding. First, I expressly thank the Commissioner for not opposing the concept of minimum requirements of the safety net in principle.
This proposal is no devil's work, nor is it a bureaucratic monster.
It is a tool for problem solving, one to which we should give a chance.
That is why I would simply like to urge support for this once again at this point.
And the second thing I would like to say, which is now directed at my British friends, across all the parties.
My dear British friends, I do understand Caroline. I do understand your problem.
I can well understand that, for a certain period of time, a country might prioritize energy security over compliance with an air pollutant limit value.
I have great understanding for that.
I am the last person who would deny Your Majesty additional time when it comes to building new coal-fired power plants.
We can talk about it, though.
The problem is, we did exactly that not.
This conversation has never taken place during the past four months.
I would ask you to remain open-minded at this point, as we now reach the second reading, and to suggest that we discuss a compromise here.
in response to the completely irrational resistance, politically unjustifiable from both a competition and environmental policy standpoint, to abandoning the minimum standards.
I believe this is the point at which a compromise can be reached, which I hope we will ultimately achieve during the second reading.
Incidentally, I do not believe we should refrain from seeking a compromise during a first reading.
This legislation is too complex for that purpose.
I do not wish to conclude this legislative term as rapporteur and leave the next Parliament with legislation involving a legal text that is inconsistent, illogical, and contradictory.
Therefore, I kindly request your support for the compromises when we vote here in one hour. Thank you.
Dear colleagues, I believe the crucial question regarding all the economic stimulus programs currently being put together across Europe is naturally, who ultimately receives the money?
further blank cheques will be issued to the banks, even though swift nationalization would ultimately be considerably cheaper for the taxpayer.
And should corporations and high-income earners be further relieved, despite having been fattened for years across Europe through tax giveaways?

The more money is squandered on such purposes, the more inevitable it becomes that these programs will fail, and the more likely it is that the European economy will sink further into a very dangerous downward spiral.

The years-long policy of privatization, deregulation, and liberalization—precisely this policy—has concentrated incomes ever more heavily among the top 10,000. And it was this policy that is responsible for the current crisis in which we find ourselves today.
And anyone who believes he can overcome this crisis by simply continuing exactly this policy with minor modifications has, quite simply, understood nothing at all.
The exact opposite of this is necessary.
Instead of purchasing bad paper from banks, tax funds should be used for the renovation of schools and hospitals, and for the ecological restructuring of the European economy.

Where private companies receive public funds, it must be ensured that public money is not provided without employment guarantees, and above all, that public money is not provided without the acquisition of public ownership rights, so that the state and, above all, the general public can also benefit from future earnings.
The best possible economic stimulus package currently available would be a radical redistribution of income and wealth from the top down.
The low-wage sector in Europe must be reduced, rather than continually promoted.
We need higher minimum wages. We need better social benefits across Europe.

And we need taxes that ensure that millionaires and the beneficiaries of the past financial market boom now also bear the immense losses that have occurred—and not the vast majority of citizens, who never benefited from the entire financial boom in the first place.
I believe that, at present, the socially just approach is also the only economically sensible option.
And it is the only way to break the devastating dynamics of the crisis.

Thank you, Mr. President. In early March, the political prisoners Michael Khodorkovsky and Platon Lebedev were transferred from Chita in Siberia to Moscow to be charged once again on untenable allegations.
The first public hearing day was scheduled for March 31.
On the same day, human rights consultations between the EU and Russia were supposed to take place.
These important talks have now been postponed by the Russian side indefinitely.
Russia thus clearly demonstrates the low value it places on human rights.
The judiciary continues to be used not only for purposes other than justice, but also to eliminate regime opponents, thereby deceiving the EU.
Thank you very much, Commissioner. Thank you very much, dear colleagues. I am particularly grateful to Commissioner Verheugen for once again highlighting the ban on animal testing.

has reminded me that this is now the third amendment to cosmetic legislation I have had the opportunity to accompany. The sixth amendment, the seventh amendment, and now the transition into a regulation.
And in fact, we have succeeded in banning animal experiments.
We have managed to ensure that, for example, consumers know how long a product remains fresh by simply looking at a small container with a number inside indicating how long it is still valid.

I say this also to some colleagues, particularly to Frau Reuthofer, who unfortunately was not able to join us at the beginning. Labels are never warning notices, at no point in time. If a product were not safe, it would not be allowed on the market, it would not be permitted to be marketed.
Every product on this European market must be safe and harmless.
But the labeling gives consumers the opportunity to choose,
This is democracy, and this is her right to choose.
I believe that we have very good legislation.
I have tried to make the procedure as transparent as possible.
I have tried, as Frau Kostet also mentioned, to bring together differing opinions.

because I am determined that we create legislation that is good for everyone—the consumers in the European Union, the industry that must work with this legislation, and ultimately all those who will benefit from it.
To my colleague Schnellhardt, I'd like to say once again: yes, the deodorant works differently for everyone, but the claim that it keeps you sweat-free is still being made.
And therefore, it is important that the statements regarding the depth of my eye folds after one week in Strasbourg are reasonably realistic and reliable.

I would like to once again express my gratitude to the Commissioner, particularly in regard to the statements he has made, to my fellow Members, and to my assistant, who carried out the bulk of the work.
I thank you very much.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, the report by my colleague Van Nistelrooy on the Green Paper and the future shape of coalition policy is undoubtedly one of the most significant produced by the Committee on Regional Policy in recent years.
And we agree with the fundamental concept of the Green Paper, which holds that territorial cohesion aims precisely at counteracting the polycentric development of the EU.
ensuring the overall sustainable development of the sovereign territories with their various characteristics and unique features, while simultaneously preserving their diversity.
And it is correct that in the next planning period a more comprehensive system of phased assistance for transition regions, those crossing the threshold from 75 to 100 meters,
exceeding a certain percentage of gross domestic product must be created, so that these regions can obtain a clear status and thus have greater security in their development.

And in the report by colleague Kree on obstacles encountered in the absorption of Structural Funds, the most significant shortcomings are rightly listed, which applicants are no longer responsible for in the absorption of Structural Funds.
in the use of structural funds, such as excessive administrative expenditure, too many complex regulations, and slow and cumbersome centralized administration in the member states.
Numerous recommendations are made to the Commission regarding effective measures that should be taken to overcome these obstacles.
I would simply like to highlight and emphasize two such measures.

Firstly, project managers are currently required to retain their project documentation for a period of ten years in order to have it available for inspections, which may be carried out by the Commission.
This is excessively bureaucratic for smaller projects.

Indeed, it is correct that this should now be reduced to a period of three years. And secondly, the evaluation criteria of the European Commission for innovative projects are causing significant problems.
For innovative projects, different evaluation criteria must be applied than those used for other projects.
It is simply necessary that something different takes place here.

Thank you very much, Madam President. When Europe takes money into its hands, and especially when Member States put a great deal of money into their hands to assist the automotive industry in its current crisis, it cannot be about merely securing the status quo and preventing failures; rather, it involves a range of other concerns.
The most important ones have been mentioned.
The issue is to help people find employment. They also need to secure this employment, and indeed ensure it over the long term.
To achieve this, it is necessary that we promote new technologies, innovation, and, above all, sustainability within the transportation system to a greater extent than we have done so far.

We should therefore link all our measures to these objectives, so that we do not have to endure the accusation in several years' time that we should have thought of all this many years ago, and then we would not be facing the next, new crisis.
Thank you very much, Madam President. It would be kind of you to grant me the additional half-minute that my green colleague, Frau Breyer, has just received.
The Commission had put forward a sound proposal for updating the regulation on novel foods.

Unfortunately, some amendment proposals attempt to supplement this suggestion with provisions that are incompatible with the objectives of the regulation or even seek to interfere with existing case law.
The obligation of food business operators to monitor novel foods with regard to human health and animal welfare would simply overwhelm traders.
And yet again there is an attempt to circumvent existing GMO regulations, namely the demand for special labeling of such novel foods derived from animals fed with genetically modified feed.
We have just heard it.
Fundamentally, anything relating to GMO legislation should not be addressed in the Novel Food Regulation.
I say this even though I myself, for example, had submitted a proposal to specifically exclude certain new plant varieties from the definition of novel foods.
But it cannot be acceptable that interested parties within this House misuse the Novel Food Regulation for electoral purposes.
It appears that the same applies to amendments 62 and 90, for which the Greens have requested a roll-call vote.
Who, dear colleagues, do you actually intend to put on public display?
My own political group has consistently supported the consumer's right to know what is contained in foodstuffs.
Why, then, should one not indicate a nano-origin in the ingredient declaration?
And although Amendment 62 attempts somewhat clumsily to anticipate my report on food labelling, I must tell you that I consider this labelling of novel foods to be quite compatible with my report.

I therefore recommend that my parliamentary group approve the aforementioned motions, and I will include references in my report on the Food Labelling Regulation to the section of the Novel Foods Regulation dealing with labelling.
This is possible because we have postponed the first reading on foot labeling to the next legislative term, against the wishes of the Greens.
But now it has become clear to everyone how advantageous this postponement was.
Briefly on cloning—it is animal cruelty.
We are against it.
Nevertheless, foodstuffs must be incorporated into this regulation accordingly, as otherwise they would fall outside a...
Thank you very much, Madam President.
Anyone who does not wish this man to preside over the solemn opening session of the European Parliament should support my amendment to the rules of procedure. Thank you.

Thank you very much, Madam President, Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues. Let me make one thing absolutely clear at the outset: the donation of food to those in need, particularly during these economically difficult times, is not up for debate in tomorrow’s vote on our colleague Schikirski’s report.
Therefore, I would like to make it clear right at the beginning that it was not easy to find a common line within our faction.
Therefore, respect each faction colleague's personal decision to deviate and not support the rejection of the report as we had intended.
Why cannot I accept the outcome of the committee's vote in this form?

There are a number of reasons for this, none of which have anything—absolutely nothing—to do with providing assistance to the needy within the European Union during these economically difficult times.

Madam President, it seems to me somewhat symptomatic for us—meaning the EU as a whole, but also particularly for Parliament—that we reflexively engage in discussions about situations in countries whose circumstances are, after all, not new.
It is unfortunately often the case that events occurring overnight lead us to suddenly pour forth in waves of political demands, some of which I find questionable at times.
I believe it does not reflect well on us to allow a failed aircraft attack to single-handedly trigger a general debate about Yemen.
I believe we should be clear about the fact that it would be to our advantage to devise a strategy for dealing with this situation.

I also believe that the situation in Yemen requires careful analysis, specifically the fact that it is a failed state, with a government that no longer controls large parts of the country, and we should examine for ourselves what risks for Europe arise from this.
The risks appear to be that terrorists are being trained there, and the risks also seem to be emerging along the coast of Yemen, where piracy is evidently taking place.
That is why, I believe, it is up to us—and perhaps also to Frau Eschten—to consider what we can do about these risks.
In my view, the answer primarily lies in how we can strengthen the government in Yemen, help it regain control over the country, and contain these risks.

All other discussions about the long-term development of the country must of course also take place. But I do not believe it is truly sensible, nor will we be taken seriously, if we now launch a comprehensive list of demands across all policy areas—from media freedom to women's rights—here in Parliament.
I believe this will not achieve the desired outcome in Yemen; instead, we should focus on what specific assistance we can provide in the current situation to address an urgent issue. Thank you.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that we do not take with one hand what we give with the other.

For instance, it makes little sense to channel development aid into building up agriculture in developing countries while simultaneously undermining local markets through direct or indirect export subsidies.
Likewise, no matter how vigorously we advocate for better healthcare, we undermine progress if we hinder the trade of generics or drive drug prices to unaffordable levels through patent extensions.

And while biofuels may slightly reduce CO2 emissions in the EU, they lead to deforestation and land-use change in developing countries, thus contributing to greater climate change—precisely what we sought to avoid. Not to mention the displacement of indigenous populations, loss of biodiversity, and reduction in land available for food production.
Currently, we are, that is, the EU as such, not particularly cohesive.
That policy measures should not contradict one another has been recognized by the EU in theory for some time.
The Treaty of Lisbon enshrines policy coherence for development.
Article 208 states that, when implementing political measures that may affect developing countries, the Union shall take into account the objectives of development cooperation.
We must measure ourselves against this requirement.
This year, we are reviewing progress toward achieving the Millennium Development Goals.
It is clear that without political coherence, we will not be able to fulfill our promises.

We must become aware of this responsibility, as all of us who adopt and implement laws. Before we decide on any measure, there must be an impact assessment, so that we can actually identify what negative effects European legislation might have on developing countries.
We need expertise within the Council, Commission, and Parliament that can identify inconsistencies.
The Commission's work programme on policy coherence is thus a positive step,
And now it depends on how this plan is implemented.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that in the reform of fisheries policy we must give greater consideration to the interests of local communities and must not subordinate them to the interests of European fishing corporations.
It means that we must dry up tax havens into which funds from developing countries flow.
It means that we cannot support ACTA if there remains concern that this agreement could disrupt access to medicines or technologies in developing countries.
To date, the Commission has not been able to dispel this suspicion.

Policy coherence also means that, in establishing the External Service, development policy must remain a strong, autonomous policy field, and the competences of the Commissioner for Development must be expanded, not reduced.
It also entails enabling Fairtrade in public procurement by municipalities, as my colleague Heide Rühle has proposed.
In many cases, we Members of Parliament must closely monitor the Commission and the Council to ensure they develop coherent policies.
In many cases, however, it also lies with us members of parliament to be coherent in the interest of development.
We need to improve our coordination between the committees, and we require a permanent rapporteur.
The report, which the Development Committee unanimously adopted, makes numerous sound recommendations and advances our progress significantly.
I would like to thank all shadow rapporteurs, the Ombudsman, and all the NGOs for their support and collaboration, and I hope that our joint report will also be adopted by the plenary tomorrow.

Madam President, dear colleagues, for months now we have been discussing the issue of driving times and working hours in road transport.
And I wonder what arguments will now be deployed to prove that everything we've been discussing for months is nonsense.
The most recent argument is that we urgently need to change things here, so that nothing changes.
Do you not realize, dear Frau Bauer, how absurd this argumentation has become, how thoroughly it is permeated by lies and half-truths?
This debate is nothing other than about extending the working hours of self-employed individuals.
In labour time law, however, the fundamental issue for the past 150 years has been the protection of people from excessively long working hours.
This applies both to self-employed individuals and to employees.
It makes no difference whether it is a self-employed individual who is overburdened or an employer who overburdens an employee driver.
The aim is to protect people from themselves and from excessively long working hours, and in some cases also from employers and clients.
Secondly, regarding road traffic safety, it is claimed here that nothing whatsoever could possibly happen.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are not at a hobby hardware store and we are not amateur craftsmen. We cannot legislate according to the principle of "let's just see what happens."
Everyone knows that excessively long working hours are designed to increase the risk of accidents, particularly in road traffic with serious consequences.
Third point. Who exactly do you envision as being able to monitor such a highly detailed regulation on our roads in the future?
Today, a state labor court cannot determine whether someone appears to be self-employed or actually is self-employed without extensive document review.
Stop this nonsense; it's about fair competition.
It is not a matter of setting today or tomorrow a priority for the future debate on extending working hours.
Don't play games with this house. Thank you.

Citizens care little for power games at the national level, where the issue is solely about who gets to decide,
What the citizen wants is effective decision-making, that problems are resolved.

And this is objectively the case: in the event of emergency measures, central regulation and decision-making at the European level become necessary whenever coordination among the member states is insufficient and fails to function.
This is in line with the decisions of the G20, the G8, and the heads of state and government, unfortunately not at the level of the Council.
The wheel makes him small, small, little square.
This is not an acceptable policy for citizens.
We expect the Council to take action. Thank you.

Mr. Commissioners, dear colleagues, one sometimes wonders what we have actually accomplished over the past year in terms of discussions and debates, in numerous Trilogue meetings—meetings which I could have imagined spending in far more pleasant circumstances.

And now, over a year after the European elections, when we still proclaimed in the streets and squares our intentions to bring the financial markets under control, we are actually facing a very meager result, or no result at all.
The problem has already been described.
We are dealing with markets that operate globally today.
And the member states still believe that they are capable of enforcing these rules through their national supervisory structures.
And precisely that it did not work out also contributed—not to cause the financial crisis, but to the fact that it also affected Europe.

That is why I already ask myself when this insight, which is so readily expressed in Sunday sermons, will actually be implemented in political action—when suddenly the focus shifts to protecting stock exchanges, safeguarding banking structures, and no longer protecting the citizens.

We, as the European Parliament, have taken on this task, and I hope that the Member States will also take it on, as they share responsibility for implementing proper supervisory rules for citizens, ensuring that individuals' savings remain secure in the long term.
And naturally, gentlemen Commissioners, I hope that the Commission will now truly do everything possible to achieve a positive outcome as quickly as possible, in the interest of the people of Europe.
Dear colleagues, we are entrusted in this European Parliament with the responsibility for robust regulation of the financial markets.
All that we have proposed was greatly diluted in the Trialogen.
Council and government do not wish to have strong intervention powers.
Giegold is right when he describes what is currently happening as church tower thinking.
The intention appears to be not to regulate extensively, but merely to design the system slightly less prone to breakdowns.
We will end up with a fragmented supervisory structure that will be unable to exercise genuine control over financial markets.
Insufficient lessons are drawn from this financial crisis, from this economic crisis, thereby already preparing the next one.
Many national economies, including those in Europe, will not survive the next shocks, with all the social and economic consequences for people in Europe.
They expect that this predatory form of financial market capitalism will be recaptured and that the primacy of politics will be restored.
When Unikreditbank's CEO, Alessandro Profumo, stated over the weekend that we need a strong and unified European financial supervision and a common rule for all member states,
If member states and he advocate stricter capital requirements, then this is an appeal to this Parliament to actually establish these rules.

It is the responsibility of this European Parliament to pressure the Council into urgently implementing effective regulation. Such action would merely be the first step toward establishing a status quo ante.
Finally, let actions follow the grand promises. My faction stands for strong European regulation.
Anything else, no regulation, would be a threat to the euro, to a united EU, and to democracy. I thank you.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, a poor timing for this debate.
I would have liked to see the Dutch team win once more, even if it were only for the last time in this World Cup.
Probably a bad omen, since now we're not allowed to watch anymore, and then they'll probably lose after all.

But regarding the subject, Mr. Commissioner, yes, you are giving the correct signal. Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President, we are concluding, to be precise, a package of laws today or tomorrow, which we have been discussing for more than two years.
This is not only due to the controversy, but also to an intervening European election and the push for a new EU treaty, which has led us to have to adjust certain aspects.

But I believe that if we draw a conclusion from this debate today, I arrive at the conclusion that we are now voting on a compromise that is certainly worth supporting, even if one does so with a clenched fist in one's pocket at certain points.
I would like to highlight two aspects.
One concerns the environmental requirements placed on industrial plants.
The issue here is that we need to implement the best available technology in Europe more effectively.

I believe we have found an exception clause in the current compromise that provides the European Commission, Mr. Botocznik, and that is clearly directed at you, with the necessary tool to proceed with the

In the coming years, we must pay greater and improved attention to ensuring that the approximately 52,000 industrial facilities covered by this regulation ultimately apply the best available techniques more effectively than they do today.
I have heard many arguments in this debate.
One thing I would like to address and reflect here as well, because I simply cannot understand it at all.
I have often been told that using the best available technology is too expensive and jeopardizes the competitiveness of European industry, especially during the crisis.
I would rather not recall my past, but I come from the former GDR, from eastern Germany.

The economic and ecological collapse of the GDR was also due to the fact that, over decades, investment in technology had been neglected in the years leading up to the final technological advancements.

And I believe that every industry, every industrial enterprise, is obliged to invest in the latest technological standards simply by virtue of its own existence, because otherwise it will lose competitiveness, and incidentally, this naturally also leads to a reduction in pollutant emissions.
I believe we have the opportunity today to enact a law that improves this initial situation.
The second part of the directive, and this is the more forceful aspect I mentioned, concerns large combustion plants.

Once again, as we did in the previous directive, we are discussing temporary exemptions until 2020, in some cases until 2023, for large combustion plants that are not required to meet environmental requirements, in particular air pollutant limit values.
I am addressing this message to the Council, perhaps also a little to all of us, that we must carefully consider how sensible it really is that we regularly
We welcome ambitious environmental targets, and we are open to discussing them here. But whenever it comes to implementing them, drafting concrete legislation, a tragedy unfolds across Europe.
Then it suddenly becomes clear to the member states what it costs, what bills must be paid, and in most cases, those who have made prior payments are then penalized.
After all, there are several member states that already meet the requirements today and would be left in the lurch at the end.
And I say it again here: both industrial plants and large combustion plants are essentially coal-fired power stations. Let's not talk about science fiction.
We are not discussing items that are unavailable, but rather the current state of the art.
Best Available Techniques—what is available on the market under competitive and cost considerations—can be integrated into these facilities. And against this background, we conclude today.
I am today presenting a package of legislation that I believe is better than what is currently in force, and therefore I encourage all of us to support it.

I also thank my colleagues who, over the past two years, have contributed to this process, often in a very demanding manner, for their support, their cooperation, and above all, for the fact that nearly all major factions have signed on to this compromise. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Three things have happened while we have been debating. First, it is clear that Europe will become world champion in football.
A good omen, I believe.
Who exactly it will be, we will see.
I don't want to express any preferences at all right now.
Secondly, Germany receives compliments from the UK.
Chris Davis, thank you very much. This doesn't happen every day.
It is worth noting this down.
And thirdly, the world is still in order; even among the Greens, football is more important than environmental protection.
For unfortunately, our colleague Bas Aykut has already left again.
But of course, heartfelt congratulations to our Dutch friends.
There are two points I would like to make regarding the debate that has just taken place.
First, I understand the worries and hardships of my Eastern European friends only too well.
After all, as an East German, I'm also half a Central/East European.
And I understand that it is not possible to immediately catch up in Eastern Europe with every environmental policy step implemented in the Western European countries over the past decades.
Environmental protection at a high level must be affordable.
Yes, that is correct.

But I believe that even if one takes this statement into account, it is still worthwhile to support this compromise, as it does justice to the differing initial situations in which many member states find themselves.
And secondly, the argument that the use of best-available techniques endangers competitiveness, particularly in comparison to China. This argument is not only incorrect, but actually dangerous.
We must come to terms with the fact that industrial processes in Asia are inevitably cheaper than ours.
And we will only succeed in this competition if we employ the best available technology in as many industrial sectors as possible.
Those who do not do so lose their competitiveness.
No one demands, not even this law, that an industrial facility be shut down tomorrow.
No one demands a jump from 0 to 100 in order to meet any pollutant limit within a week.
We have sufficient transition periods. However, it is incorrect to claim that one should forego this for competitive reasons.

And I believe that with this directive, we have not only made an environmental policy decision, but I also believe we have set an industrial policy course. It would be worthwhile to work on this in the coming years. Thank you.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen. I would first like to express my gratitude to the colleagues and colleagues from the other political groups for their contribution to the preparation of my report on Kosovo, as well as to Ms. Eva Mahr, who has carried out very valuable work in the secretariat here.

And to you, Mr. Commissioner, I would also like to express my gratitude for the excellent cooperation, as well as for what you mentioned—that it was important to you to travel to the northern part of Kosovo, even spending a night there, thereby clearly demonstrating to the local population that you stand with them as well.
You delivered a speech at the university and inaugurated the EU House there.

I believe this was an important signal to make clear what this European Parliament has consistently expressed in the past and is reaffirming once again today: support for an independent Kosovo on its path toward the European Union.
We are currently facing a situation in which the Kosovo once again stands at a crucial crossroads, particularly for the citizens of all ethnic groups in Kosovo.
In a few weeks, the International Court of Justice will deliver its opinion on the status of the declaration of independence made somewhat more than two years ago.

I hope that this decision, this opinion, will then provide an opportunity, so to speak, like an "open window" in English, to place regional cooperation on a new foundation, but based on self-determination.

starting from Kosovo and its independence, and making it clear that the shared future for all states in the region—Serbia, Kosovo, and all others—lies within the European Union, and that along the way toward this goal, the remaining problems must also be resolved.
It is also the text of a resolution that clearly states the future of Kosovo lies within the European Union.

I am pleased that we have a majority in favor of this, which also urges the five member states that have not yet recognized it to do so as soon as possible. That the Commission is likewise called upon once again to initiate visa liberalization as soon as possible.

because the citizens of the country, particularly the young people, must not get the impression that they are living in an isolated state, unable to move forward while everyone else in the surrounding region will soon enjoy this future.
I think this is the assignment
I hope that I will also find a large majority for this tomorrow in plenary session.

I have also submitted a new amendment that expresses strong regret over the outbreaks of violence in recent days and places great hope in swift and independent clarification, without using this as a basis for inflaming emotions over the coming days and weeks. Thank you.
Madam President, esteemed representatives, dear Commissioner, dear colleagues, the recent events in Kyrgyzstan, the outbreaks of violence, the massacre of people, the
The burning down of houses and the streams of tens of thousands of refugees fleeing their homes to escape violence have deeply shaken me and evoked sympathy for the victims.
At the same time, these events in Kyrgyzstan once again shed light on the situation in Central Asia, which must not leave us untouched here in the European Parliament.
On the contrary, we should direct our full attention to the countries in this region.

We should not focus solely on those states rich in natural resources and of particular economic interest to us in Europe, but instead consider all states equally within the framework of the Central Asia Strategy adopted in 2007.
That is, I believe, our obligation.
The Central Asian states, which only recently freed themselves from erstwhile tsarist and later Soviet rule, bear a heavy legacy.
Stalin's arbitrarily drawn borders, disregarding the ancestral regions of the peoples living there, evidently complicate coexistence both within and beyond the boundaries of these states.
Poverty, corruption, disregard for human and minority rights, authoritarian regimes, economic conditions, such as in Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan, hundreds of thousands,
Forcing labor immigration and confessing, as is currently happening in Kyrgyzstan, conflict resolution through the use of violence leave the future of these states marked with uncertainty.
Here, we must not, the European Union must not merely stand by and watch,
If we do not wish to lead these states down fundamentalist paths that would permanently expose their people to dictatorial regimes, then our full commitment is required.
In addition to the urgently needed material aid, we require contact with these states, offering active support to the democratic forces emerging there.

Let us give the Prime Minister, Rosa Otunbayeva, a chance to bring peace to Kyrgyzstan and to work toward stable political conditions. Let us allow this matter to be debated here today and put to a vote tomorrow.

Resolution must be followed by action, so that the people of Kyrgyzstan and the other Central Asian states feel that we do not abandon them on their path toward the future, nor leave them at the mercy of undemocratic forces.
The expectations placed on us Europeans in these countries are high. We should not let them down. Thank you.
The report is justified for four reasons.
The architecture of the service has numerous weaknesses, the intricacies of which will continue to concern us greatly.
The second point is that the European ambassador will manage Commission funds in the future.
This exposes the funds to a wide variety of risks, and the safeguards against these risks remain completely unclear to date.
The third point is the transfer of rights of this Parliament to a Council regulation.
We are, in this procedure, effectively co-deciding, yet we have allowed the Council regulation to preempt parliamentary rights.
Therefore, I would like to state for my file, the budgetary rules, that I do not feel bound by today's decisions.
The fourth point is the question of how we actually conduct ourselves toward one another in Parliament.

We adopted a parliamentary reform in the previous period and established procedures that we have now all disregarded, even with the approval of the presidium and also with the approval of the president.
Why are we implementing a parliamentary reform that prescribes procedures, when in practice we already violate them and fail to adhere to them when it matters?
For these reasons, I cannot approve this report. Many thanks.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Commissioner, I am very much in favor of green biotechnology, but cloning is something different.
That cannot be compared with it under any circumstances.
Cloning is animal cruelty. And many colleagues have already said so here,
I also raised this point during the discussion we had before the summer break. Only a small fraction of animal clones are actually viable.
And those who survive—of them, very, very many die very early, and in great suffering.
They suffer from malformations of limbs and organs.
They have immune deficiencies and are susceptible to illness.
This means pain and suffering, and precisely this agonizing death.
Can we really afford to take responsibility for that?
We advocate so strongly for animal welfare and then want to support such a technology.
No, we do not want this, and that is precisely why we decided in July that we do not want cloned meat or any animal products derived from cloned animals.
And that is also merely consistent.

Yes, we know that products derived from cloned animals or their offspring are likely not harmful to health, and personally, I assume they would indeed be suitable for human consumption. However, there is indeed no shortage of meat produced through conventional methods.
Why should we then engage in this cloning?
It is entirely unnecessary to carry out this animal cruelty. And the population simply does not want it either.
The public does not want these products. We do not need them either. There is no argument for cloned meat in the European Union.
And therefore, we want a clear regulation explicitly for cloned animals and yes, also for their offspring.
I want a ban on cloning and cloning products in the European Union. And I also believe that we can enforce this.
Of course, this must also apply to imports.
And I want to make sure that I am not secretly being served cloned meat without my knowledge, because it is not labeled, as has already happened here in Germany.
And I don't believe it's possible to trace back all meat.
With beef, it may be possible.
However, with pork, due to the large quantities produced, it will not be possible to trace whether it was actually ever a clone in the lineage.
Fundamentally, I think yes, it is a principle issue, it is an ethical question.
And other colleagues have stated that we must also address ethical issues, and we are responsible for ethical matters as well.
And of course there is the issue of animal welfare, but it is also a different question.
Experience shows that, as a livestock producer who has studied this field, all reproductive technologies, once they actually worked in animal production, were ultimately applied to humans as well.
And so today we must ask ourselves: will and can we accept the use of cloning technology in the not-so-distant future—and will and can we also accept it in humans?
So I don't want that, and I also can't do it.
And even if, in the future, a cloned replacement part might one day donate a life-sustaining organ to me—a clone of myself—I would not want that.
And I do not believe that this constitutes an exaggerated horror vision.
I believe that what was just mentioned would not be particularly fair in the future if we were to allow cloning.
And therefore, we urgently need a ban not only on cloning but also on cloned animals and their offspring throughout the entire EU.
And I am not afraid of the WTO.
We can implement this.
We don't need to copy everything the USA sets as an example for us, despite our transatlantic friendship. Thank you.
Many thanks, colleagues. It is no coincidence that we find it so difficult to reach agreement here in this case, as two incompatible matters are being mixed together.
We might have reached a satisfactory agreement on trademark protection if we had focused on it.
However, mixing it with material internet law is also substantively incorrect, as new and different strategies are required here compared to those in material law.
This cannot be transferred easily.
Moreover, one must be clear that every prosecution conducted online comes at the cost of interfering with civil rights, since such activities cannot be observed or detected on their own.
And the third point is absolutely clear to me. If I already impose the threat of punishment on cultural enjoyment on the web, so that I am practically always with one foot in
If I am in prison and look into the net, I will not be able to take advantage of the opportunities that the Internet also offers for art, culture, and creativity.
That is why I urge you: let's continue discussing this. That would be clear.
Thank you very much.
Mr. Chairperson, property, whether intellectual or physical, is a fundamental pillar of the market economy.
And therefore, I believe it is important that we reflect on how we can jointly regulate this issue through constructive consensus.
We definitely need a warning signal.
One-stop shop. It must become easier for consumers, self-employed individuals, and small and medium-sized enterprises to obtain the relevant licenses here.
And precisely the availability of EU-wide licenses for intellectual property rights is of particular importance.
We need a single market for intellectual property.
We particularly need the original language to be available throughout Europe.
Every language is an important cultural asset, and it should be possible to issue licenses for a language that are valid throughout Europe and thus accessible to all citizens.
We need interoperability and technical neutrality in order to use this intellectual property accordingly.
And we particularly need sanctions for those who violate competition and trade laws.
Dear colleagues, I have taken note that the Council does not wish to discuss the multiannual financial framework.

I would like to inform the Council here that the European Parliament wishes to discuss this matter, and that the Council cannot behave like a kindergarten and claim it will neither respond to our questions nor address our proposals.
Secondly, I must ask the Council in this context whether it actually wants Lisbon or whether it does not want Lisbon.
Lisbon is not merely a technical adjustment, but entails greater responsibilities, requires greater task fulfillment, and thus also involves related financial issues to be addressed.
In addition, the Council has adopted resolutions that result in new financial expenditures.
This has been carried out by many colleagues.

If the Council wishes this and if the Council wishes that no more money should be spent, then the Council must also specify precisely where it wants to achieve savings, how it intends to achieve them, and where it wants to redirect funds exactly—not merely in general terms.

suggest percentage shifts and then leave it to Parliament and the Commission to decide where to approve the resulting inconveniences, especially since our possibilities for reallocations and budgetary changes are extremely limited.

The tasks they have additionally assigned us are, however, so challenging that they do not involve minor, incidental sums, but rather I will mention only the areas concerning the financial solidity of our states and their corresponding financial difficulties.
And if the program must be utilized, these are not small amounts that could be shifted around casually.

The same applies to ITER, the same applies to issues of internal security, the same applies to major research questions, and the same applies to topics when I address priority areas that the Parliament has, namely in the fields of youth education and research.
In some cases, we are dealing with inadequate implementation. In other cases, we are also confronted with insufficient transparency in the expenditure of funds.
All these questions must be addressed and dealt with specifically, and not answered through silence on the part of the Council.
Dear Commissioner Hahn, colleagues, I have an issue with certain parts of the debate.

When discussing mountains, islands, and sparsely populated regions, I sometimes get the impression from individual contributions that these areas are extraterritorial and directly subject to the European Union.
But that is not the case.
Each of these areas belongs to a member state, which are sovereign.
And the implementation of European decisions and objectives remains, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity, the responsibility of the member states.

If I look at the European level, we have consistently paid particular attention to these areas—mountain regions, sparsely populated areas, and islands—right from the Commission's communications up to the Lisbon Treaty.
And here in Parliament, many resolutions were adopted to support this region.
Now that I hear numerous complaints about ongoing shortcomings in these regions, I am left wondering how the member states have responded to the impulses we have sent out from the European level.
And how did the regions themselves respond, and utilize the European funding opportunities?

A new strategy is being demanded here. So I already feel sorry for the officials at DG Regio, who are expected to incorporate the wide range of problems faced by islands, mountainous regions, and sparsely populated areas into a single strategy.

At the same time, we are witnessing an inflation of strategies—Danube, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, climate change, and so on—so that soon we will need a strategy for strategies in order to maintain any overview.

Dear colleagues, joking aside. I believe what is actually at stake here—something that has already been mentioned today—deserves our utmost attention, and I am very grateful to Commissioner Hahn for having highlighted it. We must fight to ensure that coalition policy and regional policy at the European level continue beyond 2013.
That, I believe, is the focus of our work, and it is precisely this aspect we should aim to convince our colleagues in Parliament of.
And as a continuation of coalition and regional policy beyond the year 2013, mountains, islands, and the other regions will also find their place.
However, we should not get sidetracked by individual motions on this topic for which there is currently no justification. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, first of all, thank you to the colleagues who have worked so intensively on this project, particularly to colleague Bilal Quadras.

has shown that in the future we can offer people 30 days of security, but also demonstrated that we are capable of exploring compromises—compromises that then lead to actionable solutions. That was

on the track, but it is now becoming clear that both the role and responsibility of the companies building the networks—those responsible for the networks—and also the flexibility of politics, which says we will not force things that ultimately will no longer make sense or be wise

are no longer reasonable. For example, flexibility regarding return flows, regarding necessary return flows, but not saying that everything must always and at any price, but also the need or possibility to assess individual cases, just as strengthening cooperation in the regional area

Thus, flexibility, solutions that keep burdens within manageable limits, and above all, the assurance that solutions exist—this is the good news from Brussels for people across Europe.
Mr. President, my dear colleagues, esteemed Members of the Commission, I need not emphasize the importance of coalition and regional policy for living together and growing together in Europe.
I also believe that future regional and cohesion policy must increasingly focus on cities.
For cities are a key driver in the development of Europe.
At the same time, however, you particularly feel ecological, economic, and social problems at this moment.
Therefore, my very specific question to Commissioner Hahn: What options do you see for highlighting urban dimensions?

More specifically, Commissioner, you recently suggested at an event here at the institution that you can envisage a certain percentage being set aside from regional funds precisely in order to highlight the urban dimension.
Are these considerations still relevant?

Thank you, Mr. Chairperson. First of all, I would like to thank Commissioner Hahn for initiating a new debate here as former Minister for Economic Affairs and Innovation, a debate which I believe can make a significant contribution to growth in Europe.
If every small and medium-sized enterprise in Europe created one additional job, we would no longer have unemployment in Europe.
We should therefore consider how we can enable small and medium-sized enterprises specifically to take on new employees and accordingly become actively engaged in international export markets.
But research infrastructure is also a key concern, just as infrastructure in the transportation sector is.
Here, I believe the Commission faces major challenges, particularly in restructuring the East-West connection—for example, Paris-Budapest—or the North-South axis.
Here, additional jobs can be created, which we currently urgently need in Europe.
Madam President, dear colleagues, there is no doubt that we have made progress in gender equality in recent years. Nevertheless, there are still many women who are pushed into low-skilled employment.
This applies not only to activities traditionally performed within the family.
In recent years, deregulation has frequently transformed social security–covered employment into atypical, precarious, and insecure working arrangements.
especially women there.
The European employment development from 2000 to 2010 included 60% new, precarious, and atypical employment relationships, predominantly two-thirds of which were women.
Women are frequently denied democratic participation due to their part-time employment or precarious working conditions in companies.
Women today, however, are significantly more engaged and better educated than women in all previous generations.
They still earn 25 percent less on average than men.
We have dealt intensively with this issue in the Employment Committee and developed sound proposals regarding the employment of women. The inclusion regardless of the status of their employment

Social security systems, the establishment of childcare facilities, aligning pensions and social systems with the goal of independent living, and organizing work according to the principles of decent work.
All of this has essentially been taken over from the report as precarious employment of women.
The Employment Committee is satisfied with the outcome of this work and requests approval of the presented report. Thank you.
Yes, thank you, Madam Chairwoman. Mr. Presidency, landmines will probably continue to pose a major problem for the foreseeable future.
Do you believe it is possible to initiate your own research programs for detecting landmines?
This is not merely a European problem; it is a global issue.
