My colleagues, in the transatlantic dialogue there is much that unites us. These are fundamental values, fundamental rights rooted in freedom and democracy. Yet there is also much that divides us—the application and concretization of these respective rights when transatlantic relations are at stake.
This contradiction becomes particularly evident whenever security issues are involved.
Data protection is a source of ongoing misunderstandings. We have known this for a long time.
Therefore, if we manage to secure an excellent agreement, this framework agreement will represent a major step forward.
We must find a solution that enables citizens on both sides of the Atlantic to effectively assert their rights with regard to the respective partner.
A second important area in which we share similar foundational principles but have entirely different approaches is climate protection.
That is why I very much wish for it to become a topic and to be discussed clearly.
The European Union must not use the transatlantic dialogue to weaken its own promises and commitments. Thank you.

To date, it remains up to national rules to determine how high fund indebtedness may be. And this means that the consequences of the crisis are not being drawn at this juncture.
There will be a race to the bottom in regulation at the national level.
What is crucial for us once again is that the looting of companies by private equity funds will also not be effectively prevented,
Of course, there are private equity investments that make sense for companies,
But the rules now adopted are unfortunately far from sufficient,

The information rights of employees, which they are rightly entitled to, as well as the protection of small and medium-sized enterprises against this form of exploitation, are not effectively ensured.
Therefore, it is very difficult to justify in this case to voters.

Unfortunately, it is the case that responsibility for this lies not with parliament, but particularly with the intensive lobbying activities of the respective industry, which have been especially successful with the British government.

Government, in the French government, but I must unfortunately say also partly in the German government, and in our parliament here we had representation of these lobbies more in the form of the liberal faction.

Many thanks once again, particularly for the cooperation here with my colleagues in Parliament.
Yes, thank you very much. First of all, heartfelt thanks to colleague Walz for the cooperation.
I can only emphasize this point: despite differing positions, it has been a good collaboration.
Many believed, and I would like to emphasize this once again, that the crisis of the euro area had come to an end.
Ireland shows that this has not been the case.
Europe continues to voluntarily surrender itself to financial markets.
The EU member states must continue to sell their government bonds on the financial markets' marketplace and win back the confidence of speculators through austerity measures.
Over the weekend, we had to observe once again where this leads.

This time it was not the allegedly fraud-prone Greeks, but Ireland—the model pupil—whose banking system, long regarded as exemplary and thus exceptionally deregulated, required rescue through the European Stability Mechanism.
Meanwhile, the financial sector is already eyeing Portugal's market capitalization, as its interest rates continue to rise further.
The ECB bears shared responsibility for this drama.
She has failed to do so and continues to fail by advocating for the issuance of Eurobonds, a civilized alternative to state gambling.
While one half of Europe is recovering from the crisis, the ECB calmly watches as the other half continues to sink deeper into poverty and insecurity.
The collapse of entire economies may suit the taste of European fund and banking crises, but this helps neither Ireland nor the monetary union nor the ECB.
Mr. Trichet, do not listen to Frau Merkel's Simplified Finance ABC.
If you want to save the economic and monetary union, don't leave European government bonds hanging in the air.
Introduce Eurobonds before the next speculative victim falls.

Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner, considering how we debated this Commission proposal during the previous legislative term—from outright rejection to enthusiastic acclaim, with all sorts of flattering remarks made about us— I must say I am delighted by what we now have before us.
And great thanks to the rapporteur and his co-rapporteurs for having produced a coherent opinion from this broad range of views.
I believe the key to this lies in focusing on the patient.
Thus, one did not take into account the many side effects, but instead asked what the patient actually needs.
I believe it is correct that we have decided the patient should be able to obtain information through various means, and not just one.
But I would like to emphasize that the physician will continue to be the patient's trusted person and will undoubtedly also influence decisions in critical matters.

Dear Commissioner, dear Minister, dear Mr. Gosses, and dear colleagues and shadow rapporteurs, first of all, I am pleased that we have now taken this step, whereby for the first time a sector of the European financial system is placed under direct European supervision.
This is also a step toward a strong Europe within a single market, and toward robust oversight of a sector that contributed to the crisis.

I would like to make one remark on this matter, dear Mr. Barnier. After all, the selection processes for the new agencies are currently underway, and the new agencies will only be as good as their personnel.

And I can only hope that in the selection of personnel, real expertise, independence from the financial industry they are supposed to oversee, a clear commitment to the public interest, will be the determining factors, and that we will not see national quotas again in the selection process, overriding these crucial criteria.
Moreover, much remains to be done in the area of rating agencies.

We still lack proper transparency regarding the information used by rating agencies. There is far too extensive use of regulatory ratings in the financial sector and in regulations governing the financial industry.
We face serious conflicts of interest; it is clear that there is market power held by three major providers, and there is no liability for ratings that are negligently produced.
Therefore, I ask you, Commissioner, it is good that the consultation is now underway and we are looking forward to the results.
And I also believe that civil society should participate in this consultation. But then it must also involve making a courageous proposal.
I am already looking forward to your proposal.
Be as courageous as you have been so far, and let us jointly enact legislation next year that truly addresses, at a European level, the issues that continue to exist in the rating market.
That is what we owe to the citizens.
Yes, thank you, Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Barnier, colleagues. The past months since the beginning of this year have shown the damage that rating agencies can cause.
There is therefore an urgent need for action.
And therefore, I welcome, or to the extent that I welcome, the directive, particularly welcoming the transfer of supervision and registration of rating agencies to ESMA.
I believe this is an important and positive step forward.
However, numerous good and further-reaching proposals have been left behind during the negotiations on the report.
And I would like to mention this once again here.
Therefore, the mandate of ESMA remains the only genuine and positive innovation.
The proposals for a public data base to facilitate the creation of ratings have been dropped. Likewise, there is no mention of establishing a public rating agency.
The consistent and ongoing review of established ratings by supervisory authorities has also been disregarded.

The Commission had continued to propose supporting unrequested ratings in order to overcome the disastrous issuer mandates for the assessment of financial market products. Nothing has come of this either.
I hope that these aspects will be taken into account in the forthcoming reports on this subject, which Jean-Paul Grosset and also you, Mr. Barnier, have mentioned. Thank you.
Mr. Chairperson, Commissioner, dear colleagues, first of all, my thanks to those who have worked on this report, particularly to my colleague Benzen.
I would like to highlight two aspects and perhaps provoke a bit of reflection.
We agree that energy efficiency, using energy sparingly, is the solution. The simplest and most reasonable one.
The question is, how do we achieve this goal?

Have we really achieved more and moved more?
Or why and at which points has energy been saved, or been used more sparingly?
that energy-intensive industries use energy more sparingly because it saves them money and is financially advantageous for them.
And I learn that people will choose this device when they know it consumes more or less energy—when they see it and can personally perceive an advantage, then they will make that choice.

Second question. The Commissioner has just very impressively and clearly explained how complicated it is merely to describe the term "What is this actually?"
How does this vary across the member states?
The colleague has just pointed out that Member States also have very different situations—economically, geographically, entirely different.
Is it then the right answer to work with a number, with a binding number, or should something else come to mind?

I believe we need to move somewhat beyond the past debates in energy policy, which rely on simplistic figures, binding targets, and supposedly miraculous solutions, as if these would achieve meaningful results. Clearly, we have not achieved enough.
Perhaps we should try the other approach, the more differentiated one, which takes into account the varying situations and states of being, and yet also achieves impact.
It is about the effects. Our state of mind does not matter.

It is important that the EU addresses the issues surrounding migration and asylum law.
The Commission has submitted corresponding proposals.
Currently, there is still no precise analysis regarding the implementation of existing legal regulations,
Hardly any mature calculations and analyses exist even for the new proposals.
I may state that the EPP unreservedly affirms the right to asylum and the right to protection for those in need of protection.
However, examining reality unfortunately also leads us to the problem that the right to asylum is being invoked for entirely different reasons or even deliberately abused.
The right to asylum and subsidiary protection is not an instrument for general immigration into the 27 member states.
We must also ensure that organized human traffickers are not given the opportunity to profit financially—into billions—from the fate of people through our asylum system.
Asylum procedures must be conducted professionally and carefully.
Those seeking protection must have the assurance of finding refuge in the EU.
Therefore, the Commission has provided for corresponding obligations in the new drafts.
I consider many of these to be appropriate, such as interpretation services, proper medical treatment, and due consideration of special vulnerability.
I would, however, also like to mention points that I consider problematic.
The authorities of the Member States are given far too few opportunities to prevent abuse.
The possibility of conducting accelerated procedures and border procedures should not be unduly restricted.

If an applicant seriously breaches their obligations to cooperate, there are almost no sanctioning options available. Quite the opposite. If he goes underground illegally, the member state should no longer be able to terminate its procedure negatively. If he reappears, he should be granted an expanded range of procedural options.
Even in cases of clearly unjustified claims, a streamlined procedure should be possible pursuant to the second subsequent application.
This means significant cost increases.
Legal aid in the form of a lawyer is also, according to the current wording of the Commission's draft, associated with a significant increase in costs for the states.

I simply and straightforwardly ask the Commission to reconsider the consequences of its proposals for practical reality, financial implications, and administrative challenges facing the member states.
We want a high standard, but it must also be feasible and must not place particularly burdened Member States in completely impossible situations.
Mr. President, thank you very much for allowing me to speak briefly once again.
I have a significant request to make of you, Commissioner Fühle.
I would like to begin by stating that false assumptions lead to incorrect cost estimates.
We currently do not have declining numbers in the area of asylum seekers, but clearly increasing numbers.
This is related to visa facilitations, for example with Serbia and Macedonia.
We are currently also witnessing a significant increase in the abuse of asylum rights in an attempt to enter a country of the European Union.

If one starts from the false assumption that opposing the initial proceeding in a higher-quality reform would save costs in subsequent instances, one automatically assumes that all member states, so to speak, have a poor initial proceeding.
This is not the case, however.
If standards are generally raised, this will inevitably result in increased costs.
An increase in quality standards for all involved parties, as well as the complexity of procedures, inevitably leads to higher costs in practical reality, though I am happy to discuss this issue.
Therefore, I once again sincerely ask you to take the concerns of the countries seriously, as practical reports from the countries are already available and being provided.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Dear colleagues, this report credits the Commission with a successful competition policy.
It must not be forgotten, however, that unregulated competition can also contribute to rising unemployment and increasing the gap between the rich and the poor.
Unimpeded competition still takes precedence over the social needs of people in the European Union.
The creed of free markets cannot obscure the fact that they not only repeatedly fail, but are incapable of establishing social justice in a lasting manner.
A few proceedings regarding distorted competition and price-fixing do not solve this problem.
A fair and simultaneously social internal market can only function with strong and fair regulation.
More is urgently needed for this.
For instance, it is not acceptable to continually subject facilities of public infrastructure to private economic competition.
The report provides guidance for the Commission's future competition policy.
Stronger consumer protection, the exclusion of public service provision from competition rules, and the control of cartels—all of these are urgently needed.
In addition, a contractual clause ensuring secure social progress would be necessary, one that stands on at least equal footing with competition rules in Europe.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is truly a significant moment that we are today commemorating for the first time in the European Parliament the Holocaust of Roma and Sinti.
And let me begin with the farewell letter written in 1943 by a 14-year-old boy, Robert Reinhardt, before his deportation from a German children's home to Auschwitz.
He wrote, I have found my parents again.
We are on the transport to the concentration camp.
I have mentally prepared myself so thoroughly that I can endure death.
I would like to thank you once again for everything you have done for me.
Greetings to all, farewell until we meet in heaven.
Your Robert.

Ladies and gentlemen, this boy, like 500,000 other Roma and Sinti, never returned. And although Roma and Sinti, alongside Jews, were among the first victims of industrialized mass murder, today, 66 years later, the full truth about this remains unspoken.
And complicity on the part of the majority society is often concealed.
We need the entire truth about this chapter of history.
The Holocaust against Roma and Sinti must be recognized and punished as a crime against humanity, so that this history may not be repeated.
We must combat the prejudices against these people with passion.
We must put a stop to racist harassment and violent acts, such as those that occurred in Hungary the year before last, and we must do so with civilian courage.
Freedom and equality and solidarity are not the privilege of individuals, but the right of all, for human and civil rights are indivisible. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I warmly welcome the outcome of the conciliation procedure.

By implementing the regulation, it will be possible to ensure that, first, a high level of protection comparable to that of other transport modes is achieved for all passengers in the coach transport sector, including those with limited mobility,

achieved, secondly, a high overall level of consumer protection has been ensured, and thirdly, due account has been taken of the specific characteristics that this is a transport sector predominantly shaped by small and medium-sized enterprises.

Thus, the excessive subsidies that would have rendered bus services nearly impossible due to excessive support have been dropped, just as have subsidies that would have excluded the majority of member states entirely from the scope of the regulation.
Public bus transportation services, which benefit the general public, must also provide a high standard of quality for disabled individuals and people with limited mobility.
They should have a right to assistance. For the sake of social integration, this should be free of charge.

Thank you very much, Madam Chairwoman. I would like to congratulate everyone, particularly my colleague Kanzian, for his efforts in developing a compromise on this difficult file, which was a challenging and lengthy process.
There were successes, which have already been listed, but there were also disappointments, such as the scope of application and other technicalities, which have already been outlined.
With this compromise for bus transport, we have secured passenger rights for all modes of transport.
The next necessary step is to establish a common, coherent, and efficient framework programme for all passenger rights.
Fair and clearly defined rights are of particular importance for the credibility of the EU among European citizens.
And to be credible, the coherence of rights for the various modes of transport must therefore be strictly respected.
There is no alternative when it comes to the rights of air passengers. Fundamentally different criteria apply compared to, for example, rail passengers.

Therefore, when developing a framework programme, it is particularly important that we do not focus on the lowest and weakest standards, but rather that we keep ambitious goals in sight, in the interest of citizens.

Madam President, esteemed Mr. President, dear colleagues, we are in the midst of a major economic crisis, and we've been discussing this for months—yes, that's true. Yet we usually address it only in headlines and then continue here cheerfully, as if nothing had happened.
I believe it is because we too in Parliament do not act concretely or honestly enough, but rather engage in headline-driven politics.
What actually do we do to promote growth and stability?
Are we ourselves also contributing to handling finances more responsibly and intelligently?
Do we really conduct a critical review of whether we are making the right expenditures, or does each of us not come up with a new idea every day about where to spend money?

If we lack the strength to concentrate financial resources on future-oriented projects—that is, conflict, debate, and decision-making—then it makes no sense to talk about innovation and research without providing the necessary means.
is relatively dishonest.
And if we want to stimulate the economy, we must also pursue a policy that promotes economic growth, rather than pondering from morning till night how to disrupt industry or the economy.
We cheerfully continue adopting resolutions, constantly demanding new resolutions today that disturb the development of the economy.
Does anyone really believe that the question of binding energy-efficient settlements, to name just one example, contributes to driving economic progress?
Do you really believe that we have caused economic damage with our decisions, which you have often made in connection with energy?
Or have we disturbed them?
And I would wish for us to honestly discuss this topic a little more, and then make fewer decisions, but perhaps those that truly move things forward.
This is more demanding, more laborious, will also mean conflict, but that is the essence of democracy.
Yes, thank you. Colleagues, the demand for a financial transaction tax is not new. For several years, opponents of globalization have been calling for such a tax.
Yet just five years ago, hardly anyone would have believed that it could be implemented in the foreseeable future.
But this week it is on the agenda of the European Parliament alongside a CO2 tax and euro bonds, and I think that is just fine.
I can only welcome and support the financial instruments proposed by Anni Podimata in her report, and I believe we will vote on them accordingly.
They are urgently necessary instruments,
The consolidation of public budgets so far has focused solely on cutting expenditures.
Cutting public expenditures, however, primarily affects workers, retirees, and the poor—those who depend on the welfare state.
The revenue side of the public sector has so far been completely overlooked.
She is the second lever that can be adjusted to consolidate public budgets.
Above all, the main contributors to the public debt crisis and the repayment of public debt can be appropriately engaged on the revenue side.
To consolidate public budgets, an increase in tax revenues is simply unavoidable.

In fact, the high level of public debt is primarily a consequence of the nationalization of private debts, namely the assumption of private banks' debts and the financing of the ensuing financial crisis by the public sector.
The states are by no means solely responsible for the debt crisis.
It is therefore not only logical but also politically necessary to hold the financial sector accountable.
With the financial transaction tax, this sector would finally be made to contribute to reducing public debt, as the main cause of it.
This is an important political signal conveyed by this report, provided it is adopted in the form in which it has been presented.
But we likewise consider Eurobonds to be a meaningful and therefore necessary instrument.
They contribute more to reducing debt than all sanctions or well-intentioned but more or less ineffective advice.

The complaints of some surplus countries that their interest burdens rise as a result of Eurobonds are, however, unacceptable, since these surplus countries simultaneously benefit from their exports to deficit countries.
This can then be criticized as a transfer union.
But anyone who wants a social Europe, anyone who wants the EU to remain united in the future, must at least原则上 agree to a transfer union.
Finally, I would like to say to the Commission that I hope it will now finally present the proposals for a financial transaction tax, which have been demanded for over a year. Thank you.

Dear Madam President, dear Commissioner Krus, radio frequencies represent a valuable and limited resource that must be managed with care, foresight, and consideration of broader societal aspects.
Therefore, the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme holds particular significance.
Radio frequencies must be used efficiently, taking into account economic, social, and cultural aspects.
To achieve this, technical possibilities must be fully utilized across all band areas to generate the highest possible efficiency.

The foundation of radio frequency policy must be the Telekom Package adopted last year. For it also established that radio frequency management at the level of the EU and its member states must equally serve cultural, social, and economic interests.
This must be particularly taken into account when harmonizing frequency usage within the EU.
In this context, I would like to point out that the implementation of the digital dividend so far has been carried out at highly varying speeds across the Member States.
A further opening up of the ORF band, particularly below 800 MHz, should therefore only be considered after taking into account the experiences gained from clearing this band.
Many technical and financial issues have not yet been conclusively resolved.

The issue of interference and the question of cost compensation for cultural institutions and local authorities during technical retrofitting—such as in the field of wireless microphones—remains unresolved to a satisfactory extent.
When allocating frequency bands, differing interests must be responsibly balanced against one another.

Comprehensive coverage with mobile broadband networks on the one hand is a fundamental requirement in the interest of European citizens. At the same time, continued development in the field of public and private broadcasting must also be taken into account as an essential instrument for information and cultural diversity.
We must set the course correctly for technological development, Europe's positioning in the field of digital information technology, and cultural diversity.

and we are experiencing rising resource and food prices, at least when one disregards the recent fluctuations.

Mr. President, dear colleague Kellam, fellow colleagues from the Baltic States, dear colleagues, today we particularly remember the expulsions and mass deportations from the Baltic States.

But just as we think of the victims from the Baltic States, we also think of the Armenians who were expelled from Turkey at the beginning of the previous century.
We remember the so-called population exchange between Turkey and Greece.
A particular peak in deportations across Europe was reached in the middle of the century under the Nazi regime and the communist regime of the Soviet Union.
The expulsions after the Second World War also inflicted deep wounds, as did the expulsions during the Yugoslav Wars.
We must also not forget that many colonial regimes were characterized by forced displacements.
We must demand a serious engagement with the various forms of displacement and the spurious justifications thereof.
And this also includes clarifying responsibility and punishing the guilty, to the extent still possible, as is currently the case, for example, in the Mladić case.
But the decisive response is European consensus, precisely through and with the independent Baltic states, and a clear commitment to continuing the still-unfinished process of European integration.
This historical responsibility is what we owe not only to ourselves, but precisely to the victims of the numerous expulsions across Europe.
Many female politicians from all countries across Europe, from East and West, have worked on it,
For example, consider the Warsaw Declaration, signed by Polish President Wozniowski and German Federal President Rauch.
And because we wish to learn from the past, we must remain vigilant, even toward initial signs of expulsions, such as in the case of the Roma.

Politics that brought so much suffering upon people in Europe, and beyond, during the past century. Never forget, never again—demands that arose immediately after the Second World War.

After the most difficult technical negotiations and political trilogues, it is now up for a vote. The political package, the major compromise between the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers on the future labelling of foodstuffs.
This regulation aims to provide consumers with more and better information about food products, enabling them to make informed purchasing decisions.
But that is not all. The European food industry should also benefit.
Legal certainty, deregulation, and improved legislation are the objectives.
This is particularly important for small and medium-sized enterprises. It should be noted that the food sector in the European Union consists of SMEs to over 80 percent.
At the same time, this labeling must be applicable to every food product and every non-alcoholic beverage. From basic staple foods to highly processed ready-to-eat meals.
That is precisely why a mandatory labeling in traffic light colors is not feasible, as this type of labeling simply does not fit on basic foodstuffs.

Despite all difficulties, despite all political and ideological differences within Parliament, and despite all national egos of the member states, the compromise now achieved can nonetheless stand up to scrutiny.
Allergens will be recognizable at first glance.
The plant origin of vegetable oils is indicated.
There is a labeling requirement for pork, poultry, lamb, and goat meat.

For further products, an impact assessment will determine whether and how origin information can be provided, for example for milk and dairy products, single ingredients—meaning products composed of a single main ingredient—and meat in processed foods.
Products such as these must be clearly marked on the front, and the same applies to formed meat and formed fish products made by assembling or gluing together small pieces.
A package may not give the impression that it contains something that is not actually inside.
If a picture of fruit appears on a yogurt, the yogurt must actually contain fruit, not just food coloring and flavoring agents.
In addition, the mandatory declaration of the main nutrients is required, always per 100 grams or 100 milliliters, for reasons of comparability.
Added water in meat must be indicated, the date of first freezing for meat and fish, and many other details.
Above all, all information must be legible.
We have defined readability, established a minimum font size, and instructed the Commission to set binding rules for further factors, such as the contrast between the text and the background.
At the same time, we protect craftsmanship from excessively high costs and bureaucracy.
Unpackaged fresh products are expected to provide allergen information, but not the complete nutritional information that would require expensive analyses.

Thanks to the constructive cooperation of all shadow rapporteurs as well as many colleagues, we have for the first time succeeded in developing a unified, comprehensive, and coherent approach to improving road traffic safety through this report.

It takes into account the contents of the UN Global Plan for the Decade of Action for Road Safety 2011–2020 as well as the objectives and activities of road safety efforts by EU member states and broader sectors of civil society.
It includes, first, ambitious but realistic goals for the period up to 2020 and, beyond that, the vision Sirum for the subsequent period.
Accordingly, the number of accident fatalities is to be reduced by 50 percent by 2020, the number of killed children by 60 percent, and the number of seriously injured persons by 40 percent.
This is in the fundamental interest of each and every one of our people, as well as in the interest of our national economy.
We recommend that Member States break down objectives and methods to the level of their territory and expect them to develop their own road safety action programmes.
Secondly, it specifies who is responsible for the individual activities and measures.
This is by far not only the European Union.

In line with the principle of subsidiarity, the holistic approach means that Member States, companies responsible at regional or local level, indeed every individual road user, must assume responsibility in accordance with their respective duty to cooperate.
And thirdly, it encompasses how, that is, with which instruments, activities, and measures, the objectives are to be achieved.
Legislative proposals at the European level are only one strategy.
We cannot and will not do without guidelines, recommendations, and directives.
Particular attention has been paid to subsidiarity. This means that responsibilities and competences have been clearly defined, even in cases where they are shared.
We appreciate the Commission's diverse activities.

Since they originate from a wide variety of directorates-general, such as Transport, Economic Affairs, Consumer Protection, Environment, Communication, Research and Development, the Internal Market, and others, they are not always coherent, often uncoordinated, and sometimes even contradict each other.
Therefore, we propose the appointment of a coordinator for road safety within the European Commission.
This avoids the establishment of an agency for road traffic safety in a sustainable manner, but greatly increases the efficiency of the European Commission.
Both the selection of measures and their evaluation require a scientifically sound approach based on comparable, high-quality data, definitions, and statistics.

We therefore call, among other things, for a harmonized analysis of injury and accident causes, as well as the exchange of data across the EU, while maintaining a high level of personal data protection.
Six main action areas have been identified.
