Do what you must do quickly. Do not let yourself be slowed down. Regulate every product, every institution, and every player as Frau Möller did.
Merkel has said. But a transitional government alone, which enforces the financial policy emergency program, cannot have a future if there is no democracy-political emergency program.

And this means, in this case, of course we need a European community, of course we need other agreements, of course we need a functioning institution—but it only gains legitimacy and legitimacy only if there are popular referenda.

And for this, we must now campaign, as the situation has become so dramatic that a fundamental change of mindset is urgently required—before the right-wing forces, before the national divisive factions, regain control over something that had already been built.
Thank you very much, Madam Chairperson. There is no doubt that this esteemed House stands for transparency and openness toward the citizen.
My group in the EVP also does this, of course.
However, the present report goes far beyond the actual objective.
Existing legislation and interinstitutional agreements are being ignored. The protection of competition procedures, sensitive data, and personal data is being disregarded.
This seriously violates data protection, which we ourselves consistently advocate so strongly elsewhere.
Shall we now betray the privacy of the world public?
Precisely this danger exists with the present report, as administrative procedures and decisions—including, for example, personnel matters—are to be made public.
The same applies to the protection of trade secrets and sensitive information in the context of approval procedures, court proceedings, and competition proceedings.

With its spongy, unclear, and overly broad definitions, the report permits public access to such documents. Indeed, any data and content that are in any way connected to the policies, measures, or decisions of all EU bodies and institutions are defined as documents.
This also includes largely preparatory, confidential, and secret documents, as well as all information related to them.
And thus, this definition encompasses not only document but also information.
Information from ongoing interinstitutional procedures, such as informal trilogue negotiations, should also be made public.
Do you know, Mr. rapporteur, really, what this means?

It means public access to the procedure. It means public influence on the decision-making process. Then we might as well simply seat lobbyists at the table and let them negotiate. Is that really what the citizen wants?
I can't imagine it.
In addition, documents relating to non-legislative procedures and internal administrative measures are also to be published. Where, then, is the public interest?
Or would it not rather hinder our work?
I believe this report did not come about in the spirit of our citizens.
He would bring about a flood of useless information.
He would make our work impossible, and above all, as you heard from the commissioner, he is unacceptable to the other institutions because he violates existing rules and laws.
You know that, Mr. Rapporteur, and fundamentally, you are working against greater transparency and against the citizen. Thank you.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, with such an important and comprehensive topic as the White Paper on the future of transport, the risk is always great that in the end we will have a Christmas tree, where everyone tries to fit in their two or three wish topics.
When examining the outcome set to be voted on tomorrow, one can undoubtedly congratulate our rapporteur, Mathieu Grosch.

He has succeeded in developing a good, coherent, and comprehensible outcome from several hundred proposals for amendment, which sets the main priorities for future transport on safety, environmental protection, energy, and technology.
I expressly welcome the fact that important issues such as the social, health, and labor conditions of employees are appropriately addressed here.

This is particularly important in view of the impending shortage of skilled workers in the transportation sector, which requires active measures to prevent future bottlenecks in the availability of well-trained professionals.
Further opening up of cabotage in road transport only under the condition that social dumping and unfair conditions are prevented.
I also welcome the fact that here considerable importance is attached to the aspect of improving road safety.
Road safety is an issue that will continue to concern us in the years to come.
Other points I view more critically.
Thus, I would have liked a statement regarding the model ship that places greater traffic volume from roads onto railways and waterways.
And for this very reason, I consider the discussion surrounding Giga-Liners to be the least suitable solution for achieving greater sustainability.
Equally critical is the further liberalization of the railway sector and the opening up of the national passenger transport market, which should be awaited and carefully reconsidered.
Here, technical harmonization and fair competition conditions still need to be addressed.

But as I said, in this report, I hope that the Parliament's expectations here are not merely a pious wishlist, but that by 2020 a goal will have been achieved and our proposals will have become reality. Thank you.
It has been scarcely twenty years since the Central Asian states gained their statehood as a result of the dissolution of the Soviet Union.
Coming from a dictatorship, the establishment of democratic and rule-of-law structures in these states remains a lasting challenge.
Nevertheless, these states are important trading partners of the EU and its member states due to their wealth of mineral resources and raw materials.
It is therefore only logical to regulate the EU's relations with these states within the framework of partnership and cooperation agreements.
Partnership and cooperation agreements are intended to foster collaboration and lead to cooperative action, but not to unilateral stigmatization.
Child labour, and particularly child forced labour, is unfortunately still widespread in many countries around the world and must be combated.

I would, however, like to point out that it was common practice in many agriculturally oriented European regions to extend school holidays during the harvest season, allowing children from farming families to assist with the harvest—something that would not be regarded as forced labor today.
However, if there are indications of child forced labor in cotton harvesting in Uzbekistan, consequences must be drawn.

The same consequence must therefore apply to all other countries where child labour still exists, including those where we pursue economic interests of an entirely different magnitude, such as in India or China.
We should not apply double standards here.
Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, dear colleagues, I can only join those in our ranks who have extended their congratulations to the Council Presidency.
In difficult times, they were not only honest brokers, but also European brokers.

I believe it was very pleasant to experience during these difficult times, when in numerous member states we face political currents suggesting that individuals could achieve more alone than together.
and you have placed the common ground at the center
It was also very refreshing to see that you have garnered support among the people of Poland in the domestic political debate over this clear European agenda.
It wasn't exactly easy in this context.
and also the highest respect for what you have accomplished.
Over this half-year period, we have managed to make significant progress particularly in the area of financial market regulation.

If I may single out just one subject area that I myself was involved in negotiating, namely the issue of regulation regarding short selling and uncovered credit default swaps on sovereign bonds, where the Commission hesitated to propose strict regulations, where we were confronted by all

Member States were told that they would not succeed, yet thanks to the Polish Presidency of the Council, it has been possible to sanction a highly speculative instrument, and where necessary, to ban it, so that we can contribute to greater stability in financial markets.
I would like to expressly thank your Minister of Finance and the State Secretary, who have propelled this forward through a laborious process of discussion between Parliament and the Council.
This is an example of what can be achieved together in the interest of security and stability in Europe.
Mr. Steers, thank you very much for that.
Since yesterday, colleagues, the new surveillance mechanism for economic imbalances has been in effect.
Meanwhile, the specific substantive regulations are now available.
Which development does the Commission now consider dangerous? Which economic problems does it wish to see corrected?
A rise in unit labour costs in euro-area countries of more than 3% per year is considered a problem by the Commission.
A limit for decreasing wages, however, is not provided.
Cheap, dear Commission, does not mean good.
It is not enough to urge member states to achieve prosperity at the expense of their trading partners.
Although we can exploit our workers to the bone without investments in education, health, and energy efficiency, we will not thereby write stories of economic success.
Another example from the scoreboard. The loss of export market shares is to be reviewed and sanctioned.
That is nonsense.
As world trade increases, Europe's shares statistically necessarily decrease.
The indicator consequently penalizes countries that are already integrated into global trade.
Therefore, we believe the Commission should withdraw this basket and subject it to a comprehensive overhaul. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would like to begin by joining in thanking our rapporteur and all those who contributed to the report, as this is by no means routine, even when addressing competition policy for the fortieth time based on a Commission report.
I would like to thank the Commissioner very much, as 2010 was indeed the year in which you were fully responsible for competition policy for the first time, with its very comprehensive scope.

I would nevertheless like to make a request, which you have just addressed in your contribution: that we should, particularly in the area of state aid in the banking sector, achieve a certain degree of resolution
should be, as the European banking supervision is now once again calling for a new capitalization in the banking sector due to the new stress test.
and I do not believe that all this contributes to the stability of the banking system, especially when on one hand we still have a large number of unresolved or incomplete legacy issues
We have, and in fact already again, new cases arising from the pressure being built up to appropriately increase the equity ratio, particularly within the private banking sector.

I therefore earnestly request you, Commissioner, to reach a swift solution that also contributes to stabilizing the banking sector in such a way that it can meet the challenges of the international environment.
The second major issue, merger control, will continue to occupy us intensively in the coming years.
Here too, I can only urge that the clear and transparent path previously taken by the Commission within the framework of merger control be continued.

I believe it is in the interest of all parties involved, particularly consumers, that in the field of merger control, we ensure that competition remains possible, even in larger or different structures—I am not passing judgment on these structures.
In that sense, thank you all who have contributed here. And I hope, Commissioner, that we will soon reach a solution in the field of banking. Thank you.

Mr. Commissioner, I believe you have just hit the nail on the head, as we all recognize that we are capable of launching a third industrial revolution in Europe if we understand sustainability not merely as an environmental imperative but as an industrial opportunity.
Then you also spoke about resources, and that brings me to a thought I've completely forgotten today.
I actually forgot about the human resources in my office.
And Mrs. Philipp, may I please say that you worked like a horse. Excellent results—you were a central figure in this matter.
And for that, I would like to express my gratitude.
Thank you, Mr. President. To ensure freedom of speech in parliament, one also needs a functioning microphone system.
The button to turn on the microphone has stopped working for me.
I would request that a technician restore the speech function. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner, esteemed colleagues. It surprises me that we continually have to remind ourselves what is actually meant by structural and cohesion policy.

Coalition and structural policy is one of the EU's central policy areas, and by definition aims to strengthen economic and social cohesion within the Community, particularly promoting growth and employment in underdeveloped regions.
The necessity and success of coalition and structural policy have been particularly evident in the years of crisis.
In many regions, the situation looks even more difficult. As regards economic development, these funds would not be available.
The implementation of the funding structure is based on operational programs, as already pointed out, in which the funding strategy for the respective region is described.
The European Union, its member states, and regions cooperate here in a partnership, thereby creating genuine European added value.
The success of coalition and structural policy also depends on the reliability of the programs and the funds provided and made available.

This is why I am greatly surprised whenever, time and again, Member States—as well as, as we have just seen, the Commission itself—attempt to treat structural funds like a savings book to be drawn upon for any conceivable current policy initiative.

This does not do justice to the spirit and purpose of coalition politics and, moreover, undermines the actors in the regions who are already working towards achieving the goals based on long-term strategies and agreements.
Certainly, combating youth unemployment and promoting economic growth are important goals.
This will be achieved only if the collaborative partnership between the regions, Member States, and the Commission is maintained and if the actors in the regions are involved and remain involved.
Because only you possess the knowledge of what needs to be done in your regions.
Coalition and structural policy constitute the unifying, cooperative, and solidarity-based bond in the interaction among the various policy levels within the European Union.
It makes solidarity in Europe visible at the local level in the regions, and should not be abandoned carelessly due to political knee-jerk reactions. Many thanks.

Thank you very much, Mr. Chairperson, Mr. Commissioner. I support the compromise reached in the trilogue, which aims to strengthen the bargaining power of dairy farmers vis-à-vis the processing industry, although I am aware that it does not resolve all the problems currently faced by dairy farmers in certain Member States.
Already fifty years ago, Commissioner Manzold had included preferential measures within the framework of the CAP for the establishment of producer groups.
In my country, Luxembourg, dairy farming is likewise the main pillar of our agriculture due to the high proportion of grassland.
Our farmers have long understood that the best solution is to join together in a cooperative that takes on the processing of milk itself.
Five member states voted against the compromise in the Council for exactly this reason, as it does not live up to its stated purpose as a soft landing instrument.

The European Commission has the right of initiative regarding legislative proposals, and therefore I urge you to submit a legislative proposal to the Council and the European Parliament by 2012 to rectify this obvious and costly flaw in the system.

A linear reduction of the supplementary levy up to 2015, which applies to each kilogram of milk delivered, or the abolition of the fat correction, could at least mitigate the negative effects of the expiring regulation without harming member states not affected by it.
However, this milk package also serves as a final report from the High-Level Group, aimed at ensuring a smooth transition out of the milk quota system.
Remarkably, this report mentions milk quotas nowhere.
The young and ambitious farmers in Luxembourg and in four other member states who are striving to prepare for the liberalization of the milk market and expand their production,
To remain competitive on the global market after 2015, they can only do so by purchasing additional quotas now, just shortly before the end of the milk quota system.
An enormous additional and unnecessary capital expenditure, putting them at a disadvantage compared to their colleagues in 22 member states. Thank you.
Here you are, the Blue Cut. Question: Are you aware that we already have community bonds?
And that the resolution does not speak in general terms about Euro-bonds, but rather about a wide range of different options,
and are they aware that not all of these options require a contract amendment
I find it questionable that you have again isolated generalities about euro bonds here.
Exactly the degree of differentiation that we have achieved here with Mrs. Goulart's excellent proposal should not be abandoned.
I would like to hear a bit more differentiation in your statements, and I am looking forward to hearing your response.
I would just like to say briefly, dear colleague Gulda, that this is not an assessment I have made here.
I merely wish to point out that the last Italian government bond issued before Italy joined the euro had a yield of 10%.
and there was no outcry in Italy, and no one said that other countries should now take over
It was naturally understood that high interest rates had been paid.

and now suddenly, because for ten years interest rates have been low and debt has accumulated far beyond reasonable limits, because money has become cheap—both for investments and for public finances—then one wants to prolong this situation through Eurobonds.
This is a trap from which we cannot escape, and I stand by it.
Many thanks, Commissioner. Your response has genuinely disappointed me to some extent.

They evidently wish to remain with the existing system, requiring complicated negotiations for each country and thereby preventing any further access to literature, meaning that no additional opportunities for blind and visually impaired individuals will arise.
This is all the more saddening to me, as the European Union is now blocking progress within WIPO, while all or many other treaty partners would be ready to move forward.
Here, a tremendous opportunity is being missed.

They are not fighting here for greater rights for authors, but rather against people whom the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities is intended to guarantee equal access to cultural goods and the ability to fully participate in cultural life.
I appeal to you: please remove barriers and agree to a binding treaty.

Dear colleagues, the discussion taking place here in this house demonstrates that we actually represent the entire spectrum of views. On one side are those who desire no restrictions whatsoever; on the other side are those who equate every form of immigration with the threat of mass immigration.
These are the two extremes within this house.
I believe one must approach family reunification with great reason and express it rationally.
On the one hand, there are indeed problems in certain member states where overly restrictive measures are being taken.
Family reunification cannot be burdened with so many obstacles that, in the end, no one can be reunited anymore.
On the other hand, it is entirely legitimate for member states to require, for example, language skills, to demand integration, and to also require the willingness to integrate within a given state.
As a second point, Member States naturally have the right—and we should not sidestep these issues—to articulate the problems arising in connection with family reunification.

If countries such as Greece or Malta, or Romania or Italy, are no longer allowed to express the problems they sometimes face in this context, then I no longer know whether we are democratic if we simply ignore problems outright.
I do not believe we need a new guideline already at this point
I believe we should carefully discuss the issues and then consider how we can eliminate them.
The constant reissuance of guidelines does not lead to improved guidelines or their implementation.
We are currently experiencing implementation problems in part,

And I also fully understand that we first need to address the fact that we are not simply creating another new guideline or adding something on top before we have even fully discussed the previous guideline in all its complexities.
I am in favor of listening to the member states while simultaneously respecting the fundamental right to family reunification.
And if we do this with reason and sound judgment, I also believe that we will find a good path forward.
Mr. President, "Think, Sanim, think," urges the Polish aphorist Stanisław Wierzylec. And this advice would sometimes do us well in this Parliament as well.
Shortly after the resolution on the Central Asia strategy, adopted in this building, which imposed sanctions on the Central Asian states, attention is now turning to strike events in Zhanaozen
in Kazakhstan, without awaiting the results of the ongoing investigations being conducted with international involvement.

However much the deceased deserve mourning, the injured and detained individuals resulting from these events have a right to the reckoning of what occurred; nonetheless, the underlying circumstances remain obscure.

Even Vladimir Kosslov, chairman of the unregistered opposition party ALGA, stated, "That was a group of other people." The unrest was clearly not in the interest of the strikers, as it only provides grounds for resorting to violence against them.
The Central Asia expert Arkady Dubnov, also quoting, did not rule out the possibility that parts of the Kazakh opposition living abroad might also become involved.
On the other hand, it might well fit the concept of someone in the Kazakh capital, Astana, to maintain the tensions.
Finally, the power elites of Kazakhstan are already vying for succession following President Nursultan Nazarbayev.
End of quote. We had the opportunity to speak with the Kazakh opposition here in parliament, in the presence of the Kazakh ambassador.

The UN Special Representative Miroslav Jentschak announced today that he is impressed by the Kazakh government's attention to the events of Jana Ozen, and emphasized that it is necessary to uncover the true circumstances surrounding the UNRU.
We should take the Kazakh authorities at their word, judge them by their actions, but avoid reflexive pre-judgments.
As I understand it correctly, the statement you have made today is that the Commission is fighting for the continuation of the food programme for the most vulnerable people in the European Union.
She will do everything possible to present concrete proposals in the near future, a) specifying exactly where the program will be located, and b) outlining how financing will be achieved.
I consider this urgently necessary and believe that we collectively bear the responsibility to ensure that this food program actually becomes a reality.
not because I regard it as the ultimate recourse, but because, in my view, it sends a signal to people that we still possess a fundamental sense of the necessary solidarity with the

the living conditions of people in the European Union who unfortunately were unable to participate in development and have become the first and greatest victims of the manner in which the European Union has addressed the crisis.
I am thinking here of Greece, I am thinking of Spain, I am thinking of other countries where exactly what we as the European Union are doing primarily comes at the expense of the poorest of the poor.
We are making a statement, but unfortunately, it is no longer effective.
I therefore believe it is urgently necessary that we not only address the 18 million people in the European Union who currently depend on food being made available to them at
not only be made available, but rather that our focus must be on placing the fight against poverty firmly on our own agenda, at the top priority.
We have not conveyed this signal thus far.

But in my view, this is the crucial issue if we are to make further progress here and if we are to gain the trust of the citizens of our European Union, that we are genuinely building a social and solidarity-based union.
In this sense, you have our support.
We ourselves, dear colleagues, are also called upon to do something to ensure that governments, which have so far been blocking progress, must abandon this stance.
I believe this also represents a form of self-commitment for us.
Dear colleague Dohl, I have listened to you attentively.
What you are proposing—conducting a growth assessment for all expenditures alongside the subsidiarity check—amounts to narrowing the objectives down to growth.
I would like to know from you how you reconcile this with the claim of a Christian Democratic faction, which surely still has goals beyond growth.
It is generally known that the economy does not bring children into the world without raising them, nor can it provide them with a life in an intact nature.
This therefore suggests that priorities should be reviewed and potentially changed by the Commission and also by the Council.
As a faction, we will examine very closely whether what you have presented this morning actually constitutes a change in priorities.
Previously, the motto had been: first the economy, which requires functioning financial markets.
And that was initially about restoring public finances, that is, reducing debt and keeping the markets calm.
And Mr. Dohl, who is unfortunately not present here today, I actually just wanted to tell him that I would stand by it exactly as Georges Marchais did.
Although I did not understand his response, I would still disagree with him.
I believe the measures that were taken were simply incorrect.
They were wrong. It was wrong to focus exclusively on economic recovery while completely disregarding what had happened to people.
It was not right to place the stability of the euro above all else and to claim that we simply do not care what happens to people.
It must be the other way around. First, we must focus on the people; only then can we continue discussing Euro stabilization.

The speculative attacks on Spain pushed the interest rates on Spanish government bonds above 6% and beyond, bringing them close to the level at which other countries such as Greece, Portugal, or indeed Ireland had previously accepted assistance from the rescue fund.
And also the austerity policy imposed by the EU, the ECB, and the IMF on member states, with drastic reductions in all kinds of public expenditures.
Cutting wages and pensions, increasing value-added tax, and many other measures have just imposed burdens on citizens rather than preventing a new spiral.
Rigorous cutting of government expenditures, combined with a deepening downturn, drives up sovereign debt ratios. And this must be our shared understanding.
When Mr. Draghi recently stated that the European social model is coming to an end, I am pleased, Mr. Barroso, that you say no, we in the Commission do not think so.
I therefore also expect you to go to Greece, to send your people to Greece and to Portugal, to Ireland, and to precisely record what happens there.
What happens to the people who currently have to struggle for survival?
What happens or what do we do together to finally halt this suicide spiral, which has begun—particularly in the most severely affected debtor countries?
It can't be right that we stand by here and do nothing.
And social programs that might eventually take effect, once other measures have been implemented, will not help us.
We must act now. Where have your objectives from the 2020 Strategy on combating poverty gone?
What has become of the European Platform against Poverty?
It has been set aside.
That was before the crisis.
But now, right in the midst of the crisis, people need help, they need support, and they need the European Union to take action.
You don't need empty promises.
We need concrete ideas about what should be done now.
If you do so, you will receive the support of my parliamentary group,
Support all those within the Commission who have clearly recognized the signs of the times and wish to bring about change.
But please don't just carry on like this and say it will be fine anyway
Nothing will remain if things continue like this. I urge you to present a concrete project, a concrete program, showing how we can now actively assist the people in the affected countries.
Do something about it.
We will have to do so in our countries, in order to prevent cuts to the funds for the ESF 2013 or 2014 to 2020, or indeed for the other programmes as well,
that the member states fulfill their responsibilities. Thank you.
Dear colleagues, the current state of public finances and the economy in Europe leaves no room for a fairy tale about tax policy.
The situation in the Council is quite simple: very little progress is being made on tax policy proposals there.
Why?
because some countries are opposed to further tax cooperation,
You benefit from aggressive tax planning. You are tax havens yourself.
And the treaties explicitly stipulate unanimity on tax matters.
Therefore, the European Commission has repeatedly failed in the past with its proposals.
They are diluted, they make no progress, just as with the interest rate directive. And in more recent proposals, nothing ambitious has been suggested at all, out of premature obedience.

Therefore, I ask you, Mr. Schimeter, I know your job is not easy in this regard. Change your strategy, present decisive measures, and then pursue intensified cooperation to overcome the deadlock position in the Council.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, with this proposal, certainly no state bankruptcy will be prevented where it should occur, regarding the risk-sharing instruments.
But doing something is still better than doing nothing at all.
And extraordinary situations sometimes require extraordinary measures.

The budgetary and debt crisis in certain countries has led to such an extraordinary situation that key projects and measures under the cohesion and structural policies can no longer be implemented, as the banking sector is no longer available as a financier and private investors are no longer accessible.
An exceptional measure, in my view, is the proposed establishment of risk-sharing instruments to ensure continued financing of projects within structural policy.
Tools of cohesion and structural policy, which are available to countries wishing to utilize these instruments, may be used to financially secure projects already under way.
I am very grateful to rapporteur Danuta Hübner for her significant contribution to clarifying the Commission's proposal and for clearly reiterating when and how these instruments should be used.
I also believe that these risk-sharing instruments should only be justified by the current situation and must not set a precedent for the future funding period.
When a change to the Biometric Pass Regulation 2009 was proposed, requiring an evaluation after three years and the submission of four studies, our parliamentary group supported it.
Namely, also because she had repeatedly expressed major concerns on data protection grounds and due to the overestimation of the benefits of biometric data in passports.
Belief in perfect identification, the overestimation of this tool, and the resulting underestimation of other links in the security chain are truly problematic.
The handling of biometric data continues to be based on this major misconception—that they are immutable and the most trustworthy.
And this is precisely not the case.
These data are also subject to change throughout life.
In children, they appear differently than in old age.
And for this reason, reliable and reputable studies and conclusions drawn from them are necessary.
Forgery is still being carried out diligently.
we have now been able to establish.
As incidents in the United Kingdom and other countries continually demonstrate, the chips are not secure against abuse.
They are cheerfully cracked and can cause lasting damage to those affected.
Standards within the EU are also extremely varied.
The error rate is extremely high, as in France and the Netherlands.
And time and again, the question must be asked: what use are biometric data to us if documents are forged and in circulation?
If, therefore, one link in the chain is forged and the source documents are inconsistent, biometric data will not assist us further.
One final note. The Chaos Computer Club in Germany required only a few minutes to demonstrate a forgery.

To accomplish this, the friends from the Chaos Computer Club required the cap of a plastic bottle, some superglue, a digital camera, and a little wood glue. In just a few minutes, they had cracked the chip. Such is the so-called security.
We expect the Commission to submit the studies, and naturally also the results and the conclusions that follow from them. Thank you.
Esteemed Committee, the Commission is unfortunately not represented here today to the extent I had actually hoped, out of respect for Parliament.

As a Member of Parliament representing a region significantly affected by the planned changes—namely the Eifel—I am compelled, after closely following the debate, to ask whether the unfortunately rather ineffective attempt,

a genuine alignment of criteria for disadvantaged areas that truly meets the needs of these regions. Especially since the necessary information for making meaningful and responsible decisions is lacking here.
Above all, I wonder whether a sufficiently comprehensive impact assessment was actually carried out.
To do justice to farmers, we need not only biophysical criteria, but also socio-economic criteria,

And therefore, I urge the Commission to re-examine this important aspect with the necessary time, separate from the overall CAP reform package, and above all after the submission of the required documents and following a thorough analysis of the impacts through the criteria.
Indeed, I believe we must work, in cooperation with the Asylum Support Office and Greek authorities, to identify more quickly which individuals who have arrived in Greece might actually be eligible.

also be transferred to other countries within the European Union, because firstly they definitively can never return to their home country, and because secondly they may also bring with them potential and qualifications that could be meaningful and beneficial for everyone.
Above all, however, the aim is also to help the Greeks enable registration and identification more quickly, thereby accelerating aid processes, but also

to return those individuals to their home countries who neither possess qualifications nor entitlement to remain in Greece or the European Union, as they could in fact have remained in their own countries of origin.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Madam Commissioner. Thank you also to the rapporteur for her always very committed and exemplary work.
Much of the criticism expressed by Sophie Infeld is something I can share, support, or have partially supported myself.
yet there always comes a point when one must take responsibility.
And therefore, for the EPP, or indeed for myself as well, I also claim that we must now engage with realities and cannot simply retreat to ideological or theoretical positions.
The fact is that the USA have been collecting these PNR data for ten years, and there has not been a single complaint.
The fact is that we all do not want the traffic to be stopped, even with an adverse stance, that we could stop the data traffic altogether.
They had just carried it out.
It is also a fact that we do not want air traffic between Europe and the United States to come to a halt.
And it is also a fact that the United States have the right to know who is entering their country.
And in my view, the fact is also that the legal services have all verbally informed us that this agreement is better than the other agreement, than the alternatives.
Therefore, one must also take responsibility here.
and for this purpose we have also been elected
Responsibility for the citizens whom one would otherwise leave behind alone, with less protection and fewer rights.
Responsibility for the airlines, which otherwise—something the Commissioner herself had pointed out—would have been left in absolute legal uncertainty.
Responsibility also as the EU as a contractual partner regarding data protection and security, and also responsibility for maintaining a balanced relationship between security and data protection.
Therefore, we are overall in favor, taking into account that this agreement is not perfect, but precisely because of the consequences associated with supporting this agreement.
And if one wishes to better protect citizens and also provide them with improved data privacy, one assumes responsibility here and agrees to this agreement. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Thank you very much, Mr. Commissioner. Those were insightful remarks you have provided us.
Many thanks for that.

Numbers sometimes say more than long words, and to date, the European Parliament—whom you have also mentioned—along with the European Patent Office, has granted nearly 2,000 patents on plants and almost 1,200 patents on animals, developed with and without the use of genetic engineering.
I may mention two outstanding examples. The granting of patents on genetically modified and bred barley. This applies to the entire chain from seed to plant.
Therefore, our farmers are affected just as much as our brewers, producers, and we consumers.
In my opinion, this example demonstrates a devastating extent of patenting.
Second example: the so-called Long-Life tomato. Not to be confused with your Long-Life oil, which you have in your diesel engines.
Through this maneuver by the U.S. company Arcadia, which applied for the patent, it was obtained in circumvention of the valid EU regulations.
The tomato itself has not been patented, but rather its genetic material, specifically selected enzymes.

This reveals the tactics employed here. And this despite the fact, as you yourself stated, Commissioner, in the 1998 Biopatent Directive, Article 4, CLIP, it is clearly established that plant varieties and animal breeds, as well as essentially biological processes for the breeding of plants or animals, are not patentable.
We believe this definition must be clarified; it must be interpreted more precisely.
Technical methods used to support conventional breeding may not, as such, render the subject matter patentable.
We explicitly do not claim a patent on life.
And we need a clear signal here in favor of greater ethics and greater honesty in our treatment of creation, once again opposing the patentability of life throughout Europe.
Therefore, Mr. Commissioner, we demand the Commission's clear statement that patents on breeding material and genetic resources must be prohibited in Europe and remain so.
Second, we demand a clear stance on Article 4 of the Biopatent Directive. No patents on life.

And thirdly, Mr. Commissioner, on behalf of numerous farmers and breeders across Europe, we demand the clear codification of both the breeder's and the farmer's privilege throughout the entire legislative framework. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Madam Chairperson. I would like to provide my statement on the discharge for Europol and Eurojust.
Regarding Europol. There is little, if anything at all, to object to.

The annual financial statements accurately reflect the company's financial position in 2010 in all material respects, and all underlying transactions are conducted in compliance with regulations, and are therefore also lawful.
Notable are merely the high transfers in relation to the overall actually low incurred costs.
Europol attributes this to the move into a new headquarters. This is naturally understandable.
However, Europol agrees to take further measures in the future to avoid such high transfers.
In addition, Europol undertakes to monitor the implementation of the budget monthly starting in 2012.
I recommend that we maintain this, to verify whether this actually occurs.
Against this background, I recommend relieving Europol.
Regarding Eurojust: The annual report also reflects the financial situation in 2010 in all essential aspects.
All transactions are also lawful and conducted in accordance with regulations here.
However, even here, high operating expenses were carried over into the year 2011.
Following the establishment of Eurojust, this was primarily due to an organizational restructuring project, a new computer infrastructure, and costs associated with data processing.
It is announced that everything will be improved in the future, including better compliance with tender requirements.
I also recommend that we review this in the future, and above all, verify whether all open positions will indeed be filled in the future.
This has been improved.
But if these positions are not filled soon, we would almost have to consider cutting funds, which would be negative for Orius.
I nevertheless recommend relief. Thank you.
Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, what should concern and truly alarm us is not merely the sheer number of young people unemployed in Europe, but the trend itself.
Between 2008 and 2011, the number of unemployed youth increased by 26.5 percent. This is stated by the International Labour Organization.
It is this development that urgently requires action. For we are not only excluding an entire generation from the labor market, we are also cutting off an entire generation from a livable future.
If we give young people the feeling of absolute uselessness, we should not be surprised that they turn away from this EU, as they lack reasonable prospects for work and life.

Commissioner, I urgently request that you ensure decisive countermeasures are initiated. I already perceive several issues, but please make sure that the long-standing intellectual stillness does not now give way to operational chaos.

When I read that the Commission is launching a program under which young people applying abroad are now also to receive financial support, then we are putting on a traveling circus that does not address the problem.
And conversely, it is counterproductive. Skilled workers, doctors, engineers, and healthcare personnel are then lured away from countries that are wealthier and not affected by this crisis.

There is now a need in this crisis for the cause of this development to be addressed. Offensive measures are required, namely the elimination of this austerity policy, which is hampering the economy in Europe.
We need a targeted growth policy; we need to strengthen domestic purchasing power, and public investments in education, research, and alternative energies.
that would be the way
Together with European funding, it would then be possible to actually develop an integrated concept that creates a perspective on life for young people.
Final remark. This youth unemployment is also incompatible with the nonsense of wanting to raise the retirement age for people to 70.
We need a different policy to provide young people with access to the labor market.
Therefore, I urgently request this. Thank you.

Madam colleague, you have just stated that everything in favor of the financial transaction tax would amount to speculation, and yet you yourself have subsequently presented very speculative examples.

I would like to ask you, in light of what you have said, what you think of these investigations by the Commission and the Study Commission, which emphasize very clearly that, to date, one has unintentionally taken far too little account of the real economy in its dependency on

to financial markets, that therefore 95% of companies are by no means dependent on borrowing for funding, and that one can indeed turn the negative growth effect, which has been discussed so far, into a positive one by appropriately channeling growth-promoting public investment expenditures.
Is this merely speculation for you, or would you say that such a well-founded, comprehensive study by the Commission is also justified and constitutes a strong argument?

Thank you very much. Yes, colleagues, the financial transaction tax is certainly not identical to the Tobin tax, which has been under discussion for many years, but I believe it incorporates essential elements of the Tobin tax.
At the very least, she supports the idea of involving the financial markets in taxation, of taxing them, and above all of involving them in bearing the costs of the crisis.
I believe this sends a very important signal to the financial markets,
This is also a very important signal to our citizens, to our voters.

In a number of member countries, there was a wide range of actions, petitions, and written initiatives addressed to politicians calling for the introduction of a financial transaction tax at the European level.
I believe the Parliament has achieved a significant success with the majority emerging here in favor of this report and this tax.
Certainly, this is not sufficient to stabilize and constrain the financial markets as might perhaps be necessary. But I believe it is indeed a significant step that has been taken here.
And I would like to expressly appeal to the Council to take the voices of citizens, voters, seriously and to advocate for
that there will be a genuine introduction, and that the introduction of this tax will not be blocked in the Council due to the British position or other positions.
I believe it is clear that the citizens of the European Union want this tax, and I think it represents a significant responsibility.

It now lies in the hands of the Council to act here. And I would like to use my speaking time to appeal to the Council to do everything possible to ensure that this tax is actually implemented and becomes a reality. Thank you.

Dear colleagues, I believe that despite all the detailed issues which one can of course discuss and which will still be addressed in the further legislative process, we should be clear about one thing:
Be clear. Financial markets cannot function according to a model where banks pocket profits when everything goes well, but taxpayers are left holding the bag when things go wrong.
And for this reason, it makes sense to subject financial transactions to taxation as well.
And it makes sense to do so across borders.

In Europe, or at least in the eurozone, we will not experience upheavals, but rather consistently contribute to establishing a stable financial centre that generates what financial centres are actually intended for, namely that companies pay.

Access to capital, enabling savers to set aside funds or contribute toward retirement savings, so that financial markets can once again fulfill the role for which they were originally designed: adding value to the real economy.
All that drove us into the financial crisis were products that created no value for the real economy.

And therefore it makes sense to impose a tax in order to contribute to ensuring that this is severely punished, sanctioned, and regulated in such a way that capital once again fulfills its proper function.
And therefore, today I am urging strong support for this proposal with a large majority.
The reform of the generalised system of preferences was indeed necessary.
It is now good that longer durations are available and that exporters have greater security.
However, the criteria are too imprecise, as the Development Committee has already stated.
The Commission unfortunately refers only to the World Bank classification.
The Parliament has repeatedly and critically examined the gross domestic product as the sole criterion in the past.
The definition of countries with upper-middle income may refer to countries with a gross domestic product of 4,000 dollars or up to 12,000 dollars.
This is therefore an enormous and far too broad spectrum. It does not at all reflect the realities on the ground.
We certainly do not find it problematic that countries such as Saudi Arabia and Kuwait are eliminated.
The reform in this regard was therefore good.
But these countries are high-income countries anyway.
Russia and China are countries with upper-middle income levels. We certainly do not need to worry about their exclusion from the APS.
However, this category of countries also includes many poorer nations, such as Namibia and Ecuador.
Until now, the Commission had at least also taken into account the vulnerability criterion—namely, countries that export little or only very few goods.
This at least prevents countries that are less dependent on the export of various products and are therefore highly vulnerable from being overrated.
Why then did the Commission remove this criterion?
Countries such as the Dominican Republic, Fiji Islands, Gabon, and Botswana will thus be excluded, despite their economies falling under the criterion of vulnerability.
Why did the Commission not continue using the old criteria, which were already somewhat inadequate?
There is a growing suspicion that the Commission is attempting to force poorer countries into free trade agreements that are unfavorable to them.

The preference system, however, is an instrument for promoting development and cannot be replaced by purely trade-oriented instruments, in which the development path of a country or region would no longer play any role.
Fortunately, however, the colleagues in the plenary session will still have the opportunity to support our amendment, which incorporates vulnerability as a criterion.
The new regulation also results in trade regions being torn apart.
Neighboring countries in customs unions will soon have to follow different rules when exporting to the EU.
This has nothing to do with our goal of regional integration.
And the Commission should increasingly take into account development regions instead of individual countries.
We have also submitted a motion for amendment, which we hope will gain considerable support.
It is likewise problematic when countries that have benefited from the APS just yesterday are expected to fall out of the program overnight.
Transition periods are necessary, and we see in our own countries that a few months are not sufficient for economic development.

Ultimately, we Members of Parliament must also consider whether we want to focus solely on speed in all legislative initiatives and then work with lopsided compromises, or whether we would prefer to involve the entire plenary session.

I believe the entire parliament is important for legislation, and also for democracy. It is not the time or speed that matters, but rather the need for sufficient time to implement democracy and properly enact laws. Thank you.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, High Representative. I was also a member of the European Parliament's election observation commission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and I observed firsthand the great hope and expectation with which people stood in line at polling stations, eagerly awaiting their chance to cast their vote for a better future.
We now know that these expectations were not fulfilled.
Although the Congo calls itself democratic, these elections were neither fair nor democratic,
We must address this proactively, as colleague Nedeljewa has done in her report.
The former president, Joseph Kabila, has declared himself the winner of the election. The opposition is being suppressed.
In the east of the country, violent clashes between government forces and so-called rebels have flared up again, and it remains unclear whether third-party states such as Rwanda are involved.
In any case, it is the civilian population that suffers, which for decades now has been unable to live in peace and tranquility.
The country's riches are squandered in a swamp of corruption. The Congo remains among the lowest-ranked nations in all international assessments.

The international community of states, particularly the European Union with its available instruments, must increase pressure on the President of the Republic of the Congo to allow genuine democracy in his country. He will have the opportunity to do so during the upcoming regional elections.

to this end, and that the Congolese army ceases persecuting the civilian population and finally apprehends international fugitives accused of crimes against humanity, such as Bosko and Ndanganga, and brings them before a court.
Internationally, all states should finally commit to no longer supplying further weapons to the Congo.
Without external pressure, human suffering and poverty in this country will not come to an end.
Colleagues, the proposed treaty aims to establish additional enforcement powers against crisis countries within the eurozone.
The European Semester, established as a framework for necessary and improved coordination of fiscal and economic policy, is to be strengthened.
According to the Commission's intention, member states should be compelled to accept financial assistance.
The Six-Pack has already established extensive sanctioning powers that can be imposed on Euro-zone countries facing financial difficulties.
The European Semester is already perceived by citizens more as a dismantling of democracy than as a meaningful economic policy coordination instrument.
At the same time, the German government is openly threatening Greek citizens with expulsion from the eurozone should they make the wrong choice in Sunday’s election.
Why don't we stop making these humble and undemocratic threats?
In a situation where the euro crisis has once again become dramatically acute—consider Spain, Cyprus—it does not help us to put member states, which are already in crisis, on the spot.
We should finally develop solutions. The rapporteurs of TUPEX have indeed proposed reasonable approaches toward this goal.
Elisa Ferreira's mention of the repayment fund is certainly noteworthy. This would be a worthwhile and sensible measure to combat debt.
but also Jean-Paul Gauzet's proposal for an ordered state bankruptcy.
Central, however, is that Europe undertakes a joint and solidarity-based effort to get the eurozone back on a growth trajectory.
The strong countries in Europe must stop being complacent and pointing fingers at their partner nations.
You must begin doing your homework, invest in growth, and take responsibility.
Merkel's one-sided austerity policy has simply failed as a crisis solution.

Thank you, Mr. Chairperson, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues. In June 2010, the EU heads of government finally recognized that the European community requires a more closely coordinated financial and economic policy.
At the summit, they essentially invented the European Semester.
It is entirely correct that members of an economic and monetary union must not solely consider how their policies affect businesses, employees, and consumers within their own country.
A European perspective is necessary.
A single market without coordination in economic, wage, and social policy places member states under great temptation to create short-term growth and employment at the expense of their neighbors.
Such imbalances can economically, and also politically, lead to the collapse of the currency union.
And precisely such a situation is the EU currently facing.

For years, the German economy has drained its neighbors, providing no impulses through investment or wage growth. Instead, Berlin has nearly driven the eurozone into ruin with a decade of aggressive export policies.

Therefore, it is encouraging that the Commission is finally beginning to address the core issue of the euro crisis in its country-specific recommendations and is making appropriate recommendations accordingly.

It is entirely justified that the Federal Republic is called upon to invest more in education, research, and childcare. And it is entirely justified that the Commission is called upon to put an end to the abuse of mini-jobs and to align wage growth with productivity growth.
This is a late, but important insight.
And I hope, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Rehn, that you will vigorously advocate this demand before the German government, even though there is no formal procedure for surplus countries.

Mr. Commissioner, my esteemed ladies and gentlemen, colleagues. First of all, I would like to express my gratitude to the Commission, particularly Commissioner Barnier. Simply because the experience from the previous legislative term, prior to 2009,

was that, with the exception of a very brief moment of awakening when the proposal for the European Private Company was made by Mr. Commissioner McCreevy, the predecessor of Mr. Commissioner Barnier, company law had essentially been allowed to sleep deeply. And we have failed to address this important issue, which is also significant for the internal market, over these past five years.
have occurred.
Thank goodness, this has fundamentally changed.

There is now a wide range of consultation documents issued by the European Commission, along with an expert group addressing the issue of further developing company law. We currently have a very topical consultation, which we, as Parliament, of course need to address.
The Legal Affairs Committee has decided that we do not wish to present this in the form of a report. Simply because a report would require additional time and, of course, be more detailed.

We have chosen the procedure of a written parliamentary question to the Commission in order to discuss this issue with the Commission and with Commissioner Barnier, and at the same time to clearly state our position as Parliament regarding what should happen now, in a subsequent resolution.
I believe it is necessary, for many reasons, to develop a new action plan for European company law, similar to the one we previously had about ten years ago.
There are gaps within European private law. Genuine gaps that fundamentally prevent the establishment of the internal market.
I would like to mention the example of the absence of the 14th Directive on seat relocation. This is, in fact, a typical illustration of such a gap.
The Parliament has twice called in its legislative reports for concrete proposals from the European Commission to be put forward on this matter.
There is also the expert body appointed by the European Commission itself, which has likewise expressed the same opinion on this matter.
It is simply one of the fundamental freedoms in the internal market that a company must be able to move its registered seat within the internal market.
This option does not exist today, or only under extremely difficult conditions.

However, against this background, I must also simultaneously criticize the fact that the European Commission's current position on issue 14, the Directive, clearly falls short of Parliament's expectations and, in our view, does not meet the standard of a qualified response to a legislative initiative.
But we are optimistic that, as part of the ongoing work, if an action plan is indeed adopted, this proposal will hopefully be included in the action plan.
Another important aspect of company law is the question of further developing company law with the aim of reducing burdens on businesses.
We already have very concrete proposals from the Commission on the table, for example on the issue of competition.
But I believe that beyond this, there is still more to be done and further aspects that will also be clarified during the course of the consultation.
Here, we likewise expect further proposals from the Commission.
I would also like to ask the Commission to revive the issue of the European private company, which is currently stalled in the Council.

If there are individual Member States in the EU, and I may say this from my own standpoint, which on their own, due to a lack of sufficient internal resolve, are unable to overcome this deadlock here,

To advance Europe, one must sometimes consider using the instrument of enhanced cooperation here, just as is done with other initiatives such as patents or family law, so that at least all other Europeans can benefit from this opportunity.
And I would ask the Commission to give this issue renewed consideration.
Another open issue is the question of applicable law, that is, a kind of Rome Regulation for cross-border issues in company law.
I also believe that this issue warrants further investigation and discussion, and I would request the Commission to undertake this.
thus also stands in close connection
insolvency law, but of course it has already been announced that we will receive a concrete proposal from the European Commission in the foreseeable future.
You see, there is an enormous amount of work to be done in corporate law.

I am grateful that Commissioner Barnier has taken the initiative once again here, and that it is evident we will receive significant proposals during the second half of this legislative term, which can then contribute to progress in shaping the internal market.
The parliament supports this in principle and in approach.
Details will naturally be discussed further. We will certainly do so.
But thanks all the same to the Commission.
And now, of course, we would be delighted if the Commission would actually take up our initiatives and turn them into concrete proposals. Thank you.
Dear Commissioner, I greatly appreciate your proposal regarding productivity-oriented wage policy.
But when it comes to the minimum wage, you backed down compared to Frau Merkel. And I don't believe you regarding the issue of workforce expansion in healthcare.
How on earth is new employment supposed to emerge there, when the Commission has already forced southern and eastern member states to reduce their hospital capacities by as much as half?

How can employment in the healthcare sector be created if, in the new country-specific recommendations for the European Semester, the Commission is requiring certain member states to further reduce health expenditures?
I fear this point in this plan amounts to mere announcement politics, not a genuine initiative for new jobs.
On the contrary, even today, due to austerity measures, there is not only a shortage of medical supplies and medications in Greece.
I believe that anyone who continuously deploys new rescue shields for banks must now advocate for a rescue shield for people, particularly to secure medical care in crisis countries. Thank you.
Yes, thank you. The mood among countless pro-Europeans like myself is one of frightened dismay at what is currently happening in Europe.
It is nonetheless reassuring that it is now much clearer what we are dealing with, and that eyes are no longer closed even regarding Italy.
You are well aware that you are caught in a number of traps—not only those of debt and growth, but also those of trust and power. Esteemed Commission, and also addressed to the Council.
You have unfortunately so far failed to address the central issue—the growing distance between the financial sector and the real economy.
Everything we have decided here is repeatedly assessed in expert opinion as still falling short, as no serious limit is being set on shadow banking.
Moreover, we are dealing with systemic risks. The issue of Too Big to Fail remains inadequately addressed, and one hardly needs to say a word about the rating agencies.

I can only urge you and advise you, if you are now working towards the Council summit, to restore credibility in Europe regarding your serious approach to the issue of power between the real economy and the financial sector.
To put it succinctly, they must finally convince European citizens that they are genuinely working to bring banks under control and ensure that citizens are no longer forced to bear the burden alone.

Mr. Colleague Fehrmann, you come from a country that has rejected the euro, presumably on the central grounds that it wishes to preserve the flexibility of devaluation, precisely for social policy reasons.

Given the experiences you have now gained in Sweden, would you recommend that the Greeks also leave the eurozone in order to actively utilize devaluations as a social policy instrument in exactly the same way?
Yes, thank you very much, Mr. President. I would also like to sincerely thank the shadow rapporteurs and the rapporteur.
We adopted this report in the Environment Committee by a decisive majority, with 62 votes in favor, zero against, and no abstentions.
This shows that, in reality, we are actually very close together there, particularly with regard to compromises.

Of course, a high level of protection, particularly strong consumer protection, must be given top priority in these legislative processes, especially for the most vulnerable members of our society, for those in need of protection, such as children.
As for gluten intolerance, this is indeed a problem, particularly in kindergartens.
If you just take a look around, I have two children of this age, and given the prevalence of allergies and absences there, I believe we made the right choice.
And we should also discuss this together during the second reading, with the Council and the Commission.
And I believe that we will then also achieve a good outcome.
Many thanks. A report on a traffic accident over the weekend involving a truck driver who fell asleep due to exhaustion and veered into the roadside ditch has once again confirmed to me how necessary

A clear and consistent regulation of driving and rest periods, and their monitoring using a digital tachograph, is essential for us, for road safety, and also for the individuals concerned themselves.
The digital roof plan represents a certain innovation. It brings new problems, but also still some old ones.
The new problems arise from new manipulation possibilities, which we must also gain technical control over.
The old problems are those we can already observe, so to speak, through either the lack of control or insufficient control in various member states.
What we are now regulating anew, in my view, requires significant consideration. First, that automatically entering a rest period does not occur every time a journey is not recorded.
because many drivers have to load and unload, which of course also counts as working time.
The second issue is that the speed records were removed from the dossier.
I consider that incorrect, as significant accident risks also exist here.
We would also like to have weight sensors, and we would like to have a hotline that allows reporting fraud anonymously and that can be followed up on.
What I will also be submitting is a proposal that animal transport inspections be included in this regulation.
I consider this important because it is also a fundamental aspect of our legislation.

Madam President, thousands of people might still be alive today or could have avoided permanent disabilities had professional medical care been provided promptly—within the so-called golden hour—after your accident.
We would be much closer to the vision of Zero.
Well, the eCall system, which we are discussing today, cannot prevent accidents. It also does not relieve one of the duty to provide immediate assistance.
It serves solely to automatically notify a emergency dispatch center in the event of a severe accident.
This requirement must establish linguistic contact with the vehicle involved in the accident, in order to avoid misuse and overload caused by trivial incidents.
Only after the emergency call has been confirmed or no further response is received are paramedics dispatched to the accident site, equipped with only a few but life-critical pieces of information.
The responsibility for the functioning of this rescue chain lies subsidiarily with the Member States.
As long as the sensors installed in the vehicle do not detect an accident, the E-Call system remains inactive, thus transmitting no signals or data and remaining untraceable.
From a data protection perspective, it is also far superior to a mobile phone.
The system becomes active only with automatic awakening via sensors or with deliberate manual triggering.
It then transmits a predefined minimal data set of information to a rescue coordination center,
Across Europe, uniform standards and specifications will ensure that this data is recognized, assessed, and handled as an emergency call upon arrival.
Therefore, Member States must also upgrade and expand their emergency medical service chains accordingly.

We want this eCall emergency call system to be based on the EU-wide emergency number 112, to be free of charge for users, to function throughout the EU, and to be compatible with emergency call systems of neighboring third countries.
It shall be based on an interoperable technology, neutral and open system architecture.
These systems can later also be used to provide other intelligent public transport services, such as real-time traffic information or parking space availability.

In recognition of the outstanding contributions of the automotive industry in providing private emergency call systems, these systems should retain their justification alongside the public 112-passing eCall.
Users can opt for such a system based on detailed information.
This may be accompanied by costs, if applicable
use of the data for workshops, insurance companies, towing services, or other service providers
A small village in Franconia is known for its good beer. This rhyme highlights a small problem we have created for ourselves today.

Because if this brewery or the beverage distributor is 107 kilometers away from my place of residence, I no longer have any chance of them continuing to supply me with their products in the future without having to purchase a new tachograph or face additional bureaucracy.

And therefore, dear colleagues, I voted against this resolution today. I believe we should not impose excessive burdens on our small and medium-sized enterprises. Many points were included that were positive, thank goodness.
That was indeed planned: to accept only 50 kilometers within the vicinity of a regional provider.
That was completely absurd on the part of the European Commission.
Something like 100 kilometers—yes, that might just still be manageable.
But what if it goes beyond that?
Moreover, I consider it exaggerated.
And one final point, as a representative of a workers' organization within the CSU: I also say that with this regulation, we are establishing complete control over employees.
Why?
For example, the person who supplies goods to breweries for brewing or for beverages is monitored throughout the entire workday via GPS.
I therefore also consider, for data protection reasons, what we have decided today to be highly questionable, and I would like to emphasize once again why I voted against it.
Yes, heartfelt thanks for this question.
Why I am not waiting for this decision

I waited for the European People's Party's approval in the Legal Affairs Committee while we were still in the process and had already raised the necessary questions regarding legal certainty concerning ACTA.
And there was silence in the forest and refusal.
They did not want that. It is now obvious that the ECJ is being used to cause delays. That is clear.
Why I said that regarding the broad scope for interpretation,
Because you actually asked two questions, I must answer both, unfortunately.
because one can, in fact, extract and interpret surveillance of the internet, auxiliary police officers, ISPs, and everything else from these broad formulations.
And this means legal uncertainty. Legal uncertainty does not help our economy either, dear colleagues.
Mr. Leichtfried, I would like to ask you: Are you in favor of discussing the modernization of copyright law at the European level?
or are they entirely opposed to the rejection of any intellectual property?
And how do you envision the E-Commerce Directive being adapted to modern circumstances in the future?
From your statement, I understand that you reject any debate on these matters altogether and do not recognize intellectual property.
Is that correct, or do I see it differently?
Yes, thank you very much. This is not about individual facts we need to discuss, but about the fundamental question of how we stand with regard to democracy and freedom rights.
And if we examine the file, we find that it symbolically represents opacity, lack of democracy, and violation of fundamental rights, since it was secretly negotiated.
and undemocratic in a way, because after that, free use of the internet would no longer be possible, nor would the freedom to use generic drugs to the extent as before.
These are the reasons why millions of people have taken to the streets and already voted on ACTA.
Yes, a vote has been held on ACTA, on the streets, online, regarding the Petitions Committee, in numerous countries, not only outside the EU but also within the EU.
also outside the EU. And I believe we must not ignore this vote. It is a vote of direct democracy by many citizens who have elected us.
I can only tell you, file the case away, that would be decent.

Madam President of the Council, as rapporteur for both the Committee on Budgets and the Committee on Budgetary Control, I have concluded two procedures under your presidency. The budgetary rules now provide a framework for the upcoming MFF period and the OLAV procedure.
This regulation had been pending in the Council since November 2008, and you have concluded this procedure based on the outcomes of the Polish Presidency.
Therefore, I would like to thank you for two truly very dedicated finance officials who have handled these files and have also reached out to ambassadors.
They will be missed; his efficiency as a negotiator will be missed.
You have truly earned your place in safeguarding the financial interests of the European Union.
Many thanks.
Mr. President, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, I am now speaking here as a representative of my parliamentary group, and I wish to support the proposal put forward by my colleague Fjellner.
Ladies and gentlemen, we have few raw materials in Europe.
The raw material we have here is our brain and our creativity.
This requires careful handling of the issue of intellectual property rights.
The European Commission has referred this proposal, which it has submitted to us, to the European Court of Justice for an opinion in order to clarify outstanding issues.
I believe that respect for the highest court of the European Union alone requires us to await this decision.
There is, from a substantive standpoint, absolutely no reason to make this decision today.
No one will be prevented from voting against it once the court has presented its decision and its statement.
But the decision made then will take place in a different consciousness, in a different awareness than exists today.
I do not mean that ghost drivers on the internet highway should dictate our politics. And that is why I ask you to support this motion. Thank you.
As always, when colleague Turmes speaks, it is very insightful, and one would wish that what he says had much greater influence on European policy.
I wanted to follow up on that.
Of course, these project bonds are something worthwhile, but only if they are oriented toward sustainability and if the necessary political foundations are also established.
We are indeed at risk of insuring risks through guarantees that we cannot politically control.

Specifically, Germany's energy transition would require a 3,800-kilometer transmission network; currently, only 100 kilometers of these corresponding high-voltage power lines have been built, due in part to resistance from citizens—incidentally, citizens who are very environmentally oriented.
We must come up with something from politics.

One idea would be to establish shared TEN corridors, meaning not only combining high-speed rail lines and broadband networks, but also, perhaps above or alongside the motorways, constructing corresponding utility networks accordingly.
This, however, requires a far more comprehensive planning than currently envisaged, but I would like to at least raise this point here.

Dear colleagues, I consider Schengen to be one of the greatest achievements within the European Union. Conversely, I am also fully aware that the borders of a country remain a very sensitive issue for our member states and will continue to be so.
I am aware that the problems existing between the member states regarding Schengen cannot simply be ignored.
And there exist various problems.
And by ignoring them, one does not make the problems better, nor does one solve them any better.
I live on the border with the Czech Republic, and here nobody would even think of wanting to close the border simply because we are facing problems.
But I also request the Commission's support in this area, not restriction and reprimand.
The border check has been established in order to ensure security while simultaneously guaranteeing freedom of travel.

And if we successfully conduct the surveillance operations, precisely at the borders as well as with regard to the Czech Republic, where one or more drug dealers are not only involved in a few cases, but in numerous cases,

If efforts are being made to establish a transition, I believe it would be advisable for the German Federal Minister of the Interior and his Czech counterpart to meet in order to jointly address the issues existing at the border.
And the Commission should not intervene reactively, but positively and supportively.

We intend to put populists in their place, but to jointly address the real problems. That is why I also urge the Council to work together with Parliament to find a solution enabling us to successfully adopt the Schengen dossiers, so that we can ensure freedom of travel while simultaneously safeguarding the security of our citizens.
Thank you. The cases of disproportionate border controls listed by Parliament in its question also illustrate why the Council actually wants to exclude Parliament from Schengen matters.
The Council, in fact, intends to continue undermining freedom of movement without involving Parliament or any form of parliamentary oversight, without any form of public transparency.
No wonder, then, that you prefer to pat yourselves on the back in back rooms, away from uncomfortable questions.

No wonder the German government wants to completely rule out an evaluation of internal border checks without prior evaluation, thus refusing to examine just how severely freedom of movement is being restricted.

whether the Union will actually have a future, or whether it will prove, in my view, to be a historical footnote.

presidency exceptionally well prepared and that you are also fully aware of the significance of your responsibilities.
