And I can tell you here, after everything I've heard in recent years, this is the first time a President of the European Union not only does exactly that, but also announces it and states it clearly, this tone

exert, that they advocate for their rights and that they contribute to ensuring that the Council, that the European institutions are fully aware of this task.
We continually discuss the euro and its stability, as well as the necessity—something I also recognize—that it plays a key role in the functioning of the European Union and the eurozone.
But that is an instrument.
For this, political conditions have existed, which have been defined.

It can never be the goal of the European Union to accept that people in the European Union must starve, that healthcare systems collapse, or that local administrations are forced to cease their operations, merely to sustain a functioning euro.
Something here is twisted.
It is not something we could designate as the goal of the European Union.
I strongly support your assertion that it must be about social cohesion and solidarity among the various member states, as well as among the different population groups.
And the European Union must not allow itself to be repeatedly used as an instrument or spokesperson for a small, specific segment of the population, nor may it present itself in such a manner.

And one final point I would also like to mention, of course, to emphasize once again that everything you have also named as your priorities is important to us, and that we will support the points going in this direction, as I have just defined them.
Please do something so that people no longer have fear.
Fear is the worst advisor for democracies.
And I believe that some decisions made in recent weeks and months, particularly in the elections and the referendum votes in Ireland, were also shaped by fear.
And if the leaders of the European Union contribute to people feeling afraid to make their own free decisions, then that is not a good sign for the future of the European Union.
Best of luck in your work!
Colleagues, I believe we bear a significant responsibility here, particularly as co-rapporteurs working together.

At present, eyes are focused on us and on the Council to take this step—overcoming Europe's banking crisis through another step toward European integration, namely, the establishment of a European Banking Union.
To this, it must be said that the Parliament already demanded this two years ago by a wide majority.
We have conducted 26 triads.

However, establishing the ECB as the location of eurozone supervision entails certain difficulties.

Based on the experience with the supervision package, this is not acceptable,

And therefore it is important that we work swiftly here, but also negotiate this proposal on changes to the ECB together with the necessary adjustments to the existing supervisory framework as a package, thereby effectively establishing the principle of democratic involvement of Parliament.

Thank you. By proposing to exclude developing countries from preferential market access, the Commission attempts to force these countries into free trade agreements.

Countries such as Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Namibia would be severely affected by this restriction.
Regional integration projects such as those in Southeast Africa would collapse if Kenya were to enter into such an agreement.

We should align ourselves with this in the plenary session.

is unsuitable for strengthening a European community, a community based on solidarity.

Thank you, Madam President. The new directive establishes a framework for handling books, newspapers, artworks, films, musical compositions, and television productions that are protected by copyright, yet whose rights holders cannot be identified or located.
Accordingly, books, films, photographs, or other intellectual creations for which no right holder can no longer be identified may also be made available to the public.
And thus, a cultural treasure is uncovered.
For us in the Committee on Culture, it was particularly important to emphasize the special significance of the diversity of the cultural heritage of the European Union in this legislative initiative.

In the archives of our museums, libraries, publishers, and broadcasting organizations, countless works remain unused; and simply because their rightful owners are unknown, they have so far not been made available.
This changes with the directive,
It will enable future use of this cultural asset, provided that a thorough search is conducted beforehand.
This is a very important point, so that it is not casually stated that we cannot identify the rightful owner.
For a balance had to be struck between the existing rights of creators of works and the public's need to be able to use such works where the rightful situation cannot be clarified.
A particular focus for us also lies in ensuring that both known and unknown rights holders are fully and adequately protected going forward.
And where rights holders cannot be identified, the draft makes the use of digitized orphan works possible.
Projects such as Europeana and other major European digitization initiatives, as well as our museums, archives, educational institutions, and public broadcasting, will be sorted.
In the future, it must also be about making orphan works in the archives of private publishers and the public domain accessible.
In my view, this draft guideline and the compromise now reached truly represent a compromise and a first step in the right direction.
And I believe that the cultural treasure that is at hand holds immense potential.
We should continue working on this when revising the directive in two years' time. Thank you.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I believe that today's debate on MiFID touches upon the core area of financial market regulation.
this legislative term, the issue is about regulating financial markets, not merely focusing on individual products as we have done in the past.
I would like to mention only a few brief keywords.
We didn't make things very easy for each other in our work last year.

We held a hearing in the committee, conducted interviews with all stakeholders and interested groups, and based on this, developed a result through various processes, working documents, and similar means, which we are presenting to the plenary session for voting today and tomorrow.
I would like to highlight a few points.
Theme number one, market structure.

On the one hand, we must naturally acknowledge that considerable developments have taken place in the field of financial markets in recent years. This is our concern, but as a Parliament, we require that every trading of financial instruments takes place within the MiFID rules. Which is why we also support, under many conditions, a new category,

have approved the so-called Organized Trading Facilities, where only non-equities are to be traded, where proprietary trading is to be prohibited, and where, in accordance with execution in respect of execution, transactions, and customer interaction, appropriate execution may take place at their own discretion.
I believe that with this we are making an important contribution toward ensuring that the entire segment of OTC instruments traded falls within the scope of the MiFID framework.
Another major issue, which was not addressed in the previous MiFID, is so-called algorithmic trading or high-frequency trading.

Here too, our aim was to establish clear rules of the game, ensuring that this area— which can be extremely dangerous and is also susceptible to manipulation—is subject to strict regulations including minimum retention periods,

Possibilities for trading interruptions, enabling the testing of algorithms, allowing supervisory bodies to examine algorithms, introducing a so-called tick-size regime with a minimum price increment, and implementing appropriate fee structures.
Another topic I would like to address is the field of commodity futures markets.

I do believe we bear a particular responsibility here in ensuring that the speculative element in genuine futures markets is restricted to such an extent that we do not contribute—through incorrect regulations—to the situation where people in the third and fourth worlds can no longer afford to buy food.
That is why we have adopted a two-part approach, aiming to curb speculation while avoiding harm to the real economy.
With position limits, with strict upper bounds on the number of contracts or positions, we aim to achieve this.
We also want to ensure, through a so-called position-check system, that those who can demonstrate they genuinely need a product do not suddenly start speculating in this market.
Another topic that occupies us greatly is the area of investor protection.
I would like to point out here only that we have adopted a very broad approach when it comes to ensuring consumer and investor protection.
The aim here is to identify the needs of specific customer groups,
Not every small saver needs access to all highly complex documents.

The focus is on defining appropriate products for target groups, conducting product testing, telephone recording, providing appropriate advice, and fulfilling disclosure obligations. But, and I want to briefly highlight this as well, applying the principle of subsidiarity in the area of commissions.
Member States should have the opportunity, according to their respective structures, to make appropriate adjustments.
I think we are on the right track.
Once my time is up, I would like to thank everyone who has contributed here.
I find it regrettable that one is given only four minutes of speaking time for such a complex dossier.
I would like to thank all my colleagues who have contributed here, as well as the Commission for its very constructive role over the past weeks and months.
And I hope that with a strong mandate from the plenary tomorrow, we will be able to enter negotiations with the Council and conclude this important file as soon as possible. Thank you.
Dear colleagues, thank you first of all for the generally good cooperation on this report.
We have been able to further improve a Commission proposal submitted at key locations, which had already taken significant steps in the right direction.
In the area of market structures, there will be greater transparency, with more comprehensive transparency, particularly in the field of derivatives trading.

within the framework of MiFID. The decisions made in the area of high-frequency trading, our compromise, go far beyond what the Commission originally proposed and deserve the support of the Parliament as a whole.
And even regarding the issues that have provoked significant public criticism, the report points in the right direction.

In the area of commodity and food speculation, after extensive and difficult debates—something that must also be acknowledged—the restrictions are now significantly stronger than the Commission's proposals, as position limits are now being established as binding.
We do, however, still need further changes in this area to close remaining gaps.
And I also hope for further input here during the plenary vote, and ultimately during the trilogue.
And it is encouraging that Mr. Barnier has already signaled his support for the Parliament's more far-reaching proposal.
Where the report, however, has weaknesses is in the area of consumer protection.
We now have the possibility to ban certain products, which is a step forward; however, we still have no changes in the area of commission-driven advice.
The distribution of financial products remains largely not oriented toward the interests of customers, but is strongly dominated by the extent of commissions available for each product.
Consumers have lost a great deal of money, not only in Germany but throughout the EU.
Consider the open-ended real estate funds.
For citizens in countries such as France, Italy, Spain, and Germany, very little will change even after our compromise, unfortunately.
The fundamental principle will remain further commission orientation.
Referencing subsidiarity here is a bit too simplistic.
Here, I would have preferred stricter regulations.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, yes, what lasts a long time ultimately turns out well—I can only say that.
The report on the type approval of vehicles in the L category has ultimately turned out to be a good report after all.
The work took longer overall than any of us had expected, and in particular longer than we had hoped. And there were far more pitfalls than ultimately suited us.
But at this point, I would like to sincerely thank the rapporteur, Mr. Vandekamp, once again for the truly cooperative and constructive collaboration.
Nevertheless, we have achieved a great deal together in the negotiations with the member states.
As rapporteur for my political group, I recommend approving this report. That said, it could have been much better.
And by better, my very esteemed colleagues, I mean safer.
For us Social Democrats, the safety of vehicles—and thus the safety of drivers—has been a priority from the outset, particularly the safety of scooter riders and motorcyclists.
The number of motorcyclists involved in accidents has remained constant for years.
Generally, the number of accident fatalities in Europe is declining. Only not in the case of motorcycle accidents.
Reducing the number of motorcyclists and moped riders killed is indeed a matter close to my heart.
Fewer deaths on European roads—this has been and remains my goal.
Every dead person is one too many.
This issue must be thoroughly addressed, particularly in a regulation so advantageous for the internal market.
I remain convinced that this can be achieved through effective technologies of anti-lock braking systems, namely ABS.
And I am equally convinced of the cost-effectiveness.
It is simply not true that ABS is too expensive.

Nevertheless, we have achieved that from 2016 onward, all new motorcycle types and from 2017 onward all existing motorcycle types with an engine displacement of 125 cc and above must be equipped with ABS by the manufacturer as a mandatory requirement.
Motorcycles may optionally be equipped by manufacturers with ABS or combined braking systems.
In this respect, we must unfortunately admit that we have not achieved any improvements over the Commission's proposal.
I had hoped for much more here. But the Commission and the Member States have, to put it mildly, shown themselves to be very sluggish.
We have nonetheless achieved that in 2019 the Commission presented a report.
The report must be based on traffic accident data from the Member States and should examine the mandatory installation of ABS on motorized two-wheelers.
It is also sent to the Parliament and the Council.
On the basis of this report, the Commission will then consider making the installation of ABS on mopeds mandatory.
You see, the subject remains very, very alive.
Therefore, we will endeavor to close the binding gap in legislation starting in 2019.
I am certain that the collected data will support my position.
I am convinced that this is indeed a good day for motorcycle riders across Europe, and that accident figures will decrease in the future. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Matthias Grote, there is no technology without risks. There is no perfect energy generation technology.
and I believe this also applies to what we are discussing here today.
I am strongly in favor of our discussing this in a rational manner.
What we are currently experiencing is a new entrant into energy markets through unconventional resources, primarily in gas, but also to some extent in oil.

I believe we must keep in mind that these unconventional resources have the potential to transform all energy supply relationships we currently know, between suppliers and demanders, and that the economic viability of previously established energy sources is now being called into question.
we should confront the development

because I consider a period of one year between being notified that the complaint has been received and possibly receiving a response so late to be extremely lengthy.

have jointly decided. Then we must inform those who are responsible, those who are genuinely engaged in a European manner, as thoroughly and precisely as possible.

Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, five years ago the EU's Central Asia strategy was launched.
When asking today for an assessment of the outcome of this strategy, the evaluation is ambivalent.
We see how the member states individually strive for good economic relations, particularly with the resource-rich countries in this region. On the other hand, these efforts are not making progress.
To accompany states in their transformation process toward modern democratic societies, or as the recent events in Kazakhstan show, development can even be marked by setbacks.

Nevertheless, or precisely because of this, the European Union and its member states must more strongly express their influence in this region together, especially and particularly towards Kazakhstan, which has now become a major supplier for numerous EU countries.

Isolating Kazakhstan from the EU would merely enable competitors in terms of influence in this geopolitically crucial country—those less concerned with enforcing civil and human rights—to further strengthen their influence than before.
The linkage of economic interests with the promotion of civil rights is the central element of the Central Asia strategy.
This should also serve as the basis for further negotiations on a partnership and cooperation agreement with Kazakhstan.
The present report provides useful guidance on this matter. Many thanks.
Ladies and gentlemen of the absent Council, perhaps you are following us via the Parliament’s video stream, as you evidently have neither the capacity nor the inclination to speak with us live.
I regard the absence of the Council here and today as a serious disregard of Parliament.
Rather than avoiding this debate, you should instead reveal your secret reason for opposing any improvement in the protection of personal data in the areas of police and justice through a directive,

For years, police and judiciary have been, as we know, an Eldorado for data protection violations. Not only in the case of SWIFT, but also, for example, on a daily basis, by unlawfully collecting and storing data on citizens—such as those who demonstrate against Nazis.
We need identical protection standards for the public and private sectors, as well as for police and judiciary. This is about ensuring the guarantee of civil rights.
